PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION - FIRST DISTRICT
NOTE REGARDING DEPRECIATION ACCOUNTS
The Uniform System of Accounts prescribed by the Commission for street
railway companies provides that operating expenses shall include under
the head of maintenance the estimated amount of capital consumed, which
is defined as "the amount estimated to be necessary to cover such wear and
tear and obsolescence and inadequacy as have accrued" during the fiscal
period. Until otherwise ordered, such estimated deterioration is based on
a rule determined by the accounting corporation and filed with the Com-
mission. From the total estimated deterioration during the period, there
is deducted the actual amount expended in repairs, and the remainder is
charged to depreciation or credited if the actual amount expended for repairs
exceeds the estimated deterioration. Three depreciation accounts have been
provided in operating expenses, 'Depreciation of Way and Structures,"
Depreciation of Equipment," and "General Amortization "; amounts
charged to these accounts are concurrently credited to the account “ Accrued
Amortization of Capital," the official title of the depreciation reserve.
6.
66
Under paragraph 30, where the term "amortize" is first used in the
accounting order, there appears the following explanatory note:
66
NOTE.- By "amortization" of any charge or credit is meant its gradual
extinction. The word is broader than the word depreciation," since the
latter is restricted ordinarily to tangible property. The word "depreciation
also imports more of the idea of fluctuating value and is complicated some-
what with the question of cost of replacement at market prices. Because
it is considered unnecessary in connection with the gradual consumption
or expiration of life of capital to consider the question of cost of replace-
ment until the replacement is actually made, at which time the cost of
replacement is duly charged to the appropriate account, and because pro-
vision is necessary for the gradual extinction of certain charges (such as
those for some kinds of capital, those for extraordinary casualties, for dis-
count on debt, etc.), to which the term "depreciation" does not well
apply, it is considered advisable to use the term "amortization in connec-
tion with the extinction of such charges, and of certain corresponding
credits, such as premiums on debt outstanding.
The text of the depreciation accounts is as follows:
(730.) DEPRECIATION OF WAY AND STRUCTURES.
Each month the sum of the amounts charged for that month to the fore-
going repair accounts in Maintenance of Way and Structures shall be com-
pared with the amount estimated to be necessary to cover such wear and
tear and obsolescence and inadequacy as have accrued during the month on
all way and structures of the accounting corporation. If the estimated
deterioration exceeds the total repairs, the account Depreciation of Way
and Structures" shall be debited and the reserve account "Accrued Amorti-
zation of Capital" shall be credited with an amount equal to the excess.
If the sum of the repairs exceeds the estimated deterioration, the account
"Accrued Amortization of Capital" shall be debited and "Depreciation of
Way and Structures" credited with an amount equal to the excess. Where
capital is substantially continuous, such as tracks, and can not be satis-
factorily individualized, the capital shall be kept in efficient operating con-
dition through repair, and the renewals and replacements of parts thereof


## p. (#239) ################################################

STREET RAILWAY COMPANIES
239
shall be considered repairs. In the case of buildings, towers, bridges, tres-
tles, and other separate structures capable of being readily individualized,
charges to this account must be sufficient to provide (in respect of such
capital) in the account "Accrued Amortization of Capital" by the time
such structures go out of service a reserve equal to the original cost thereof,
less salvage, to which account such original cost, less salvage, may be
charged, except as provided in account (No. S100) "Fixed Capital, Decem-
ber 31, 1908."
NOTE. Until otherwise ordered, the "amount estimated to be necessary
to cover such wear and tear and obsolescence and inadequacy as have accrued
during any month shall be based on a rule determined by the accounting
corporation. Such rule may be derived from a consideration of the said
corporation's history and experience during the preceding five years and
the accrual may be on the basis of revenue car-miles. Whatever may be
its basis such rule and a sworn statement of the facts and expert opinions
and estimates upon which it is based shall be filed with the Public Service
Commission on or before July 1, 1909; each amendment of such rule and
a sworn statement of the facts and expert opinions and estimates upon
which such amendment is based shall be filed with the Public Service Com-
mission before it is used by the accounting corporation, and shall show the
date when it is to be effective. Such rules and statements shall be filed
upon sheets 8 x 11 inches or 9% x 12 inches and shall be entitled "Rule
of the (here naming the accounting corporation) concerning Depreciation
of Way and Structures."
(Uniform system of accounts for street and electric railways, page 54.)
(760.) DEPRECIATION OF EQUIPMENT.
Each month the sum of the amounts charged for that month to the fore-
going repair accounts in Maintenance of Equipment shall be compared with
the amount estimated to be necessary to cover such wear and tear and
obsolescence and inadequacy as have accrued during the month on all
equipment of the accounting corporation. If the estimated deterioration
exceeds the total repairs, the account "Depreciation of Equipment" shall
be debited and the reserve account "Accrued Amortization of Capital"
credited with an amount equal to the excess; if the sum of the repairs
exceeds the estimated deterioration, the account "Accrued Amortization of
Capital" shall be debited and “ Depreciation of Equipment" credited with
an amount equal to the excess. The net amount charged (or credited) to
this account for any month, and concurrently credited (or charged) to the
reserve account "Accrued Amortization of Capital," must be sufficient, ex-
cept as provided in the account (No. S100) "Fixed Capital, December 31,
1908," to provide in that account, in respect of the several items of equip-
ment by the time such items go out of service, a reserve equal to the
original cost thereof, less salvage. When any capital is retired from service,
the amount (estimated if not known) originally charged to a capital ac-
count in respect thereof shall be credited to such capital account, and
(except as provided in the account "Fixed Capital, Dec. 31, 1908") the
original money cost of such capital less salvage, if any, shall be charged to
the account "Accrued Amortization of Capital," any necessary adjusting
entry being made in the proper account under Corporate Surplus or
Deficit."
66
NOTE. Until otherwise ordered the "amount estimated to be necessary
to cover such wear and tear and obsolescence and inadequacy as have accrued


## p. (#240) ################################################

240
PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION - FIRST DISTRICT
during" any month shall be based on a rule determined by the accounting
corporation. Such rule may be derived from a consideration of the said
corporation's history and experience during the preceding five years and
the accrual may be on the basis of revenue car-miles. Whatever may be its
basis such rule and a sworn statement of the facts and expert opinions and
estimates upon which it is based shall be filed with the Public Service Com-
mission on or before July 1, 1909; each amendment of such rule and a
sworn statement of the facts and expert opinions and estimates upon which
such amendment is based shall be filed with the Public Service Commission
before it is used by the accounting corporation and shall show the date
when it is to be effective. Such rules and statements shall be filed upon
sheets 8½ x 11 or 92 x 12 inches and shall be entitled "Rule of the (here
naming the accounting corporation) concerning Depreciation of Equipment."
(Uniform system of accounts for street and electric railways, page 59.)
(S842.) GENERAL AMORTIZATION.
Charge to this account at the close of each fiscal period and credit to the
account "Accrued Amortization of Capital" such amount as is attributable
to the period in respect of amortization of intangible capital not assignable
to Maintenance of Way and Structures or to Maintenance of Equipment.
This includes such matters as amortization of limited franchises, amorti-
zation of organization, etc. which shall be based on rule to be filed with the
Public Service Commission on or before July 1, 1909.
(Uniform system of accounts for street and electric railways, page 68.)
(374.) ACCRUED AMORTIZATION OF CAPITAL.
Credit to this account such amounts as are charged from time to time to
"Operating Expenses," or other accounts to cover depreciation of way and
structures, depreciation of equipment, and other amortization of capital.
When any capital is retired from service, the original money cost thereof
(estimated if not known, and where estimated, that fact and the facts
upon which the basis is estimated shall be stated in the entry.), less salvage,
shall (except as provided in the account "Fixed Capital, December 31,
1908") be charged to this account. The amount originally entered or con-
tained in the charges to any capital account in respect of such capital so
going out of service shall be credited to such capital account, and any
necessary adjusting entry made to the appropriate sub-account under the
account Corporate Surplus or Deficit."
66
(Uniform system of accounts for street and electric railways, page 31.)

Continental Insurance Co., 14,000; Charlotte B. Wilbour, 12,000; United States Trust

States Fire Insurance Co., 8,009; Gertie A. Garman, 7.500; Westchester Fire Insur-
ance Co., 7,500; Robert Goelet, 7,000; Philomena A. Curron, 7,000.
The company controls through direct stock ownership the Brooklyn City Develop-
ment Corporation.
Important Changes during Year.- Investments made during the year, and held at
close of year, were as follows: Brooklyn City Development Corporation stock, par
and cost $345,000; U. S. 6% certificates of indebtedness, par and cost. $100,000; real
estate bonds and mortgages, various, $74,000. U. S. Liberty bonds, 44's, par value,
$88,000, cost, $86.453.40 were sold March 5, 1920. for $78,765; loss, $7,688.40. The
Rengan Realty Co. mortgage for $24,000, the Davidson mortgage for $19.500 and
the Felder mortgage were transferred to the Trustees of Insurance Reserve
Fund. The Sherrill mortgage for $10,000 was also sold. [The lessor company also
reported the sale of U. S. Liberty bonds, $20.000 44's for $19.128, at a loss of $872.
and U. S. Victory notes, $30,000 44's for $29,832, at a loss of $168. Real estate mort-
gages showed a net decrease of $69,250.]

The Brooklyn City Railroad Company
Agreements were made as follows: With L. M. Garrison, Receiver of the Brooklyn
Heights R. R. Co. whereby the Brooklyn City R. R. Co. will take possession of its
properties and take the place of the Brooklyn Heights R. R. Co. in all its contracts,
agreements, etc., other than trackage agreements, and generally providing for opera-
tion and power, dated and approved October 16, 1919, which contract was consented
to by L. M. Garrison as Receiver of the other companies of the B. R. T. system, and
by the other companies of that system not in receivership. The transfer was effec-
tive from midnight October 18.
With the Receiver of The Nassau Electric R. R. Co. for the lease of 13 trailer
cars, dated October 19, 1919.
With the Receiver of the Brooklyn Heights R. R. Co. for the use of 54 trailer cars
and 108 safety cars, dated December 22, 1918; assigned, April 22, 1920, to the Brooklyn
City Development Corporation.
With the New York Consolidated R. R. Co. for the exchange of transfers at
Fourth avenue and 86th street, dated October 31, 1919.
With the Post Office Department, March 25, 1920, effective April 1, for the trans-
portation of letter carriers and special delivery messengers, 1,155,000 tickets for
which $38,500 is to be paid in quarterly installments - one-half each to The Brooklyn
City R. R. Co. and Lindley M. Garrison as receiver and agent; and under verbal
agreement this amount is apportioned among the various companies on the basis of
the actual number of such tickets honored by each company.
Under the order of the Public Service Commission in Case No. 2431, dated Novem-
ber 19, 1919, the collection of a second fare on the Flatbush avenue line was dis-
continued, but court proceedings were pending.
No new powers or franchises were acquired.

THE BROOKLYN HEIGHTS RAILROAD COMPANY
Incorporated April 1, 1887. The Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company on January 24,
1896, acquired control of the company by purchasing its capital stock from the
reorganization committee of the Long Island Traction Company.
Important Changes during Year.- Lindley M. Garrison was appointed Receiver
by the United States District Court, effective July 14, 1919. The lines leased from
The Brooklyn City Railroad Co. were returned to that company October 18, 1919,
upon abrogation of the lease. New investments were made by the purchase of
U. S. certificates of indebtedness, 5½%, $400,000 at par and Second Liberty Loan
44% bonds, $9.000 par for $8,096.63.
Agreements or contracts were made as follows: With J. G. Brill Co., covering
108 single-truck safety cars, July 29, 1919.
With the Coney Island & Gravesend Ry. Co., October 16, 1919, terminating the
car lease dated July 1, 1914.
With the Nassau Electric R. R. Co., October 16, 1919, terminating the car lease
dated August 24, 1908, and transferring obligations under the lease of July 1, 1914.
With The Brooklyn City Railroad Co. whereby The Brooklyn City R. R. Co. will
take possession of its properties and take the place of the Brooklyn Heights R. R.
Co. in all contracts, agreements, etc., under condition of lease dated February 14,
1893; likewise in the maintenance agreement of February 28, 1907.
With Receiver of Nassau Electric R. R. Co., October 19, 1919, a lease to Nassau
Electric R. R. Co. of ten centre-entrance cars, Nos. 3070-5079.
With Brooklyn City R. R. Co., December 22. 1919, leases to Brooklyn City R. R.
Co. of 54 trailer cars, Nos. 6000 to 6053 and 108 safety cars, Nos. 7000 to 7107.
With the Brooklyn, Queens County & Suburban R. R. Co., Coney Island & Brooklyn
R. R. Co. and Nassau Electric R. R. Co., June 1, 1920, covering temporary arrange-
ment to operate Bridge local cars on the Williamsburgh Bridge.
With Receiver of Nassau Electric R. R. Co.. June 15, 1920. leasing to Brooklyn
Heights R. R. Co. 9 motor cars and 9 trailers for operation on the Williamsburgh
Bridge.

BROOKLYN, QUEENS COUNTY AND SUBURBAN RAILROAD COMPANY
Incorporated November 24, 1893. The company is controlled by the Brooklyn
Rapid Transit Company, which purchased a majority of the stock on January 24,
1896. Lindley M. Garrison was appointed Receiver and begun operation of the
respondent's property July 14, 1919.
Important Changes during Year.- Agreements were made as follows: July 29,
1919 with J. G. Brill Co. covering the purchase of eight safety single-truck cars,
consideration $44,800; December 20, 1919, between American Coin Register Co., Hubert
Perry and L. M. Garrison as Receiver, covering 10 Vogelsang fare boxes; March 25,
1920, with the United States Government (by Postmaster General), for the trans-
portation of mail carriers and special delivery messengers for one year from April
1, 1920, superseding agreement dated July 1, 1917; consideration is $38.500 for
1,155,000 tickets, payable quarterly July, October, January and April, one-half each
to the Brooklyn City R. R. Co. and L. M. Garrison as Receiver. Under verbal
agreement the amount is apportioned among the companies on basis of actual num-
ber of tickets honored by each company at 3% each; May 25, 1920 between South
Brooklyn Railway Co. and Receiver of Brooklyn, Queens Co. and Suburban Rail-

BROOKLYN RAPID TRANSIT SYSTEM
The Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company is a business corporation which has con-
trol of various street railroad companies through stock ownership as shown in
previous Reports. Through merger of the Transit Development Co., August 1, 1918,
it owns power plants and facilities, which are pooled with those of the subsidiary
companies. By virtue of other operating agreements, also published in previous
Reports (especially that of 1917), it undertakes the maintenance work of the sub-
sidiary companies. The cost of power and maintenance as reported for the fiscal
year 1920 is shown in the following statements:

THE CONEY ISLAND AND BROOKLYN RAILROAD COMPANY
LESSEE OF DE KALB AVENUE AND NORTH BEACH RAILROAD COMPANY
Incorporated December 10, 1860. The majority voting power in this company is
held by the Coney Island and Gravesend Railway Company, through purchase of
capital stock on January 15, 1914. The respondent is lessee of the De Kalb Avenue
and North Beach Railroad Company, which it controls through ownership of en-
tire capital stock.
On July 14, 1919, Lindley M. Garrison as Receiver began operation of the com-
pany's property.
Important Changes during Year.- Agreements were made as follows: July 29,
1919, with J. G. Brill Co., covering the purchese of 11 single truck safety cars, con-
sideration $61,600; April 8, 1920, with the Brooklyn City R. R. Co., and Lindley M.
Garrison, as Receiver of the Nassau Electric R. R. Co., for the operation of the
Park Slope Line on Coney Island avenue from Avenue C to 15th street, to 9th avenue
(Prospect Park West), to Flatbush avenue, to Fulton street, and thence back to
Court street and Livingston street to Flatbush avenue; June 1. 1920, with M. L.
Garrison, acting as Receiver for the Nassau Electric R. R. Co., Brooklyn Heights
Railroad, and the Brooklyn, Queens Co. & Suburban Railroad Co., covering tem-
porary arrangement to operate Bridge Local cars on the Williamsburg Bridge.
Effective August 1, 1919, the charge of two cents each for transfers was au-
thorized by the Public Service Commission. In March, 1920, the company acquired
$16,000 United States 2nd Liberty Loan 44% bonds cost $14,393.20. Track mileage
was decreased .128 miles, consisting of .027 siding removed on De Kalb avenue and
.101 mile abandoned on River street from South 1st street to Grand street. For
contract with the Post-Office Department see abstract of the Brooklyn City R. R.
On July 14, 1919, Lindley M. Garrison as Receiver began operation of the company's
property. No new powers or franchises were acquired.
Fixed Capital Installed since Dec. 31, 1908.- Additions within the year: Other
street railway land, $13,750; ties, $15.40; rails fastenings and joints, $145.17; special
work, $1.55; track laying and surfacing, $75.25; paving, $108.88: interlocking and
other signal apparatus, $217.21; distribution system, $80.76; revenue cars, $1,013.26;
electric equipment of cars, $38.82; total, $15,446.30.
Expenditures for Principal Projects.- Purchase property Center and Walker street,
New York City, $13,750; installation additional metal fuse box, etc., on 259 cars,
$853.67; minor projects under $500, $842.63; total, $15,446.30.
Detail of Property Retired during Year from Fixed Capital Dec. 31, 1908.- Removal
of 158 feet 5 inches track construction and one-half R. H. crossover from Fulton and
Water streets, $1,320.54; removal of two No. 2 iron trolley poles from Main and
Front streets, $50; removal of 1 New Haven single register, $18; 9 Brill 22 E trucks,
$1,233, and 2 Brill 21 E single trucks, $600. Accounts Credited.- Track and roadway
construction, $1,320.54; electric line construction, $50; cars, $1.851; total, $3.221.54.
Equipment Retired during Year from "Fixed Capital," since Dec. 31, 1908.- None.

EIGHTH AVENUE RAILROAD COMPANY
Incorporated January 10, 1855. [On November 23, 1895, the property and franchise
rights of the company were leased for 99 years from January 1, 1896, to the
Metropolitan Street Railway Company, which latter company was succeeded
January 1, 1912, by the New York Railways Company.]
The twenty principal stockholders as of January 5, 1920, and the number of
votes to which they were entitled were: Anna W. Peter, 2,225; Estate of Anna
F. Wright, 626; Estate of Sara Williams, 624; Alga Law, 611; Trustees M. P. C.
Blossom, 508; George Henry Warren, 475; Mary L. Hays, 405; Mary J. Lasala, 385;
Kate F. Hays, 337; Armistead Peter, Jr., 225; Ella H. Meyers, 217; F. Bianchi
Tompkins, 219; U. S. Trust Co., Trustee, 163; Henry Block, 160; Trust Estate
W. H. Tompkins, 148; Georgia W. Warren, 139; Alfred B. Maclay, 110; M. Bell
Hazen, 109; Helen Herriman, 108; Henrietta Herriman, 108.
Important Changes during Year.- Pursuant to an order of the United States
District Court dated July 15, 1919, the lease of the respondent's property to the
Metropolitan Street Railway Company was cancelled and certain of its property
was surrendered and turned over to the respondent by the Receiver of the
New York Railways Co., at midnight, July 31-August 1, 1919.
On March 31, 1920, the respondent acquired a mortgage on the property at 315
West 50th street, amount $10,000 at 5½ per cent. It purchased its own certificates
of indebtedness, $3,100. The company's property at 231-237 Hudson street and
6 and 8 Renwick street was mortgaged to the Red Diamond Realty Corporation
for $25,000, at 5% per cent, to secure a 3 year loan.
Owing to a severe storm the operation of the road was suspended from February
4 to March 18. 1920.
Fixed Capital Installed since December 31, 1908.- Additions during year: Other
street railway land, $77,600.68; 1ails, rail fastenings and joints, $9,538.32; roadway
tools, $7,932.78; general office buildings and fixtures. $5,750.61; stations, waiting
rooms and miscellaneous buildings, $70.00; shop equipment, $13.15; revenue cars,
$21,379.06; electric equipment of cars, $5,693.05; other rail equipment, $1,604.22;
miscellaneous equipment, $16,103.57; miscellaneous construction expenditures, $152.55;
total, $145,837.99.
Power Bought.- From the Interborough Rapid Transit Co., 1,231,199 kw.hrs.,
average price 2.283 cents per kw.hr., and 306,035 kw.hrs., average price 2.313 cents
per kw.hr., total price, $35,188.53; from the Receiver New York Railways Co.,
4,997,572 kw.hrs., average price 2.287 cents, and 1,286,647 kw.hrs., average price 2.313
cents; total, $144,061.71. Charged to expense, $179,250.24.

HUDSON AND MANHATTAN RAILROAD COMPANY
Incorporated December 6, 1906, under the general railroad laws of New York
and New Jersey.
Stock control of this company is held by the Hudson Companies which on June
30, 1920, was the holder of record of 251,712 shares of common and 23.076 shares of
preferred stock. Voting control has been exercised by Felix Warburg, Charles
Francis Adams, 2nd, and Albert H. Wiggin, as voting trustees under a voting
trust agreement (applicable to common and preferred stock) for 5 years nominally
expiring August 21, 1918.
Important Changes during Year. The work of waterproofing the tunnels was
continued. The mortgage on the company's property at 54 Dey street was reduced
by a partial payment from $80,000 to $75,000. Car purchase agreements were dis-
charged as follows: No. 1 (last installment paid March 1, 1919), $46,000; No. 2,
$50,000; No. 3, $42,000. The interstate fare to and from points in New Jersey changed
from 5 cents downtown and 7 cents uptown to 6 cents downtown and 10 cents
uptown, effective April 19, 1920. The property of the Company was returned to it
by the Federal Government March 1, 1920. Bonds held as investments in funds
matured during the year and were paid as follows: Property amortization fund.
N. Y. Central 5's $10,000 and Baltimore & Ohio 5's $5,000. Debt amortization fund.
Baltimore & Ohio 5's, $15,000. The profit on these bonds, aggregating $412.50, was
credited to the respective funds. U. S. Liberty bonds, $65,000, were sold at par to
amortization funds and others bought at par to the amount of $2,650. No new
powers or franchises were acquired.
Fired Capital Installed since December 31, 1908.— Additions during year: Street
railway land, other, $44,759.03; tunnels, $76.298.46; interlocking and other signal
apparatus, $13,353.88; distribution system, $6,491.66; stations, waiting rooms and
miscellaneous buildings. $586.30; furnaces, boilers and accessories, $26,934.94; sub-
station equipment, $51,692.13; shop equipment, $2,102.00; total, $222,218.40.
1 Consists of taxes for years 1909 to 1913, and 1915, $6,814.37; interest thereon,
$1,854.16.

INTERBOROUGH RAPID TRANSIT COMPANY
LESSEE
Incorporated May 6, 1902, under the laws of 1890 and 1892.
The Interborough Consolidated Corporation controls the respondent through the
ownership of a majority of the capital stock.
Respondent operates the lines of the Manhattan (elevated) Railway Company
under a lease expiring November 1, 2874. Respondent also operates the transit rail-
roads (subways) built by the City of New York, under Contracts No. 1, 2 and 3,
and related certificates, under leases each of which is for a term of forty-nine
years from January 1, 1917, or date of completion of construction.


## p. (#264) ################################################

PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION - FIRST DISTRICT
·
Interborough Rapid Transit Company
New
Sole stock control is held by this company of the following corporations:
York & Queens County Railway Company, Rapid Transit Subway Construction
Company and Subway Realty Company. Joint stock control of the Long Island
Electric Railway Company and The New York & Long Island Traction Company
is held by this company and the Long Island Consolidated Electrical Companies.
Important Changes during the Year. The following section of subway routes,
was put into operation: Pelham Bay Park line, from Whitlock avenue to East
177th street, on May 30, 1920; track mileage 6.13 and length of route 2.01 miles.
The company abandoned .05 miles of structures, tracks and turnouts at Morris
street and also abandoned the crossovers at 53rd street and Sixth avenue (.04 miles).
and at Bleecker street and Sixth avenue (.02 miles).
During the year there was deposited with the Trustee $409.370 in cash, which
was used for the purchase and retirement of $493,000 par value three-year notes.
Thereupon there was withdrawn from the collateral to the notes and used for
sinking fund purposes, $611,000 par value of the first and refunding mortgage five
per cent bonds.
The distribution of bonds issued and notes retired was as follows:

chase of $16,000 of U. S. 2nd Liberty Loan 44% bonds, and also by $1,000 in miscel
laneous U. S. Liberty Loan bonds acquired through deposit in escrow of such bonds
by the Surf Avenue Building Co., Inc. Receivers' certificates were issued Novem-

Coney Island and Gravesend Ry. Co., October 16, 1919, to lease to that company
sufficient cars for its operation. With Brooklyn City R. R. Co., October 19, 1919,
leasinsg to that company 13 trailer cars.
Car leases were cancelled as follows: November 1, 1919, the lease of 10 cars to
the Coney Island and Gravesend R. R. Co. dated July 1, 1914, and the lease of
47 cars to the Brooklyn, Queens County and Suburban R. R. Co., dated September
3, 1908.

THE NEW YORK AND NORTH SHORE TRACTION COMPANY
Incorporated August 6, 1902. February 6. 1920, the principal stockholders of
the company, with the number of votes to which each was entitled, were as
follows: Geo. A. Stanley, 2,354; W. C. Scofield, 1,170; John J. Stanley, 649: T. S.
Beckwith, 624; Fanny T. King, 624; Roy A. Rainey, 546; Louis D. Beaumont,
W. P. Palmer and Geo. F. Scofield, 390 each; Cleveland Trust Co. and David C.
Griese, 312 each; Chas. H. Stanley, 260; John N. Lanker, 234; Elizabeth S. Sweeney,
226; John S. Sweeney, 187; A. S. Chisholm, Geo. W. Crile, W. G. Pollock, W. A.
Price and Fanny S. Seabrook, 156 each.
Important Changes during Year.- Rates of fare were increased as follows: On
August 29, 1919, in New York City, 5 cents to 6 and upwards to 11 cents; on April
15, 1920, in Nassau county, 5 cents to 7 cents, 10 cents to 14 cents, 6 cents to 8 cents,
12 cents to 16 cents and 20 cents to 28 cents. Operation was suspended on the entire
road May 3, 1920, and May 8 foreclosure proceedings were instituted by the trustee
for the bondholders.
No new powers or franchises were acquired.

NEW YORK CONSOLIDATED RAILROAD COMPANY
LESSEE OF NEW YORK MUNICIPAL RAILWAY CORPORATION (GRANTEE UNDER RAPID TRANSIT
RAILROAD CONTRACT No. 4)
Incorporated November 30, 1912. This company controls through ownership of its
entire capital stock the New York Municipal Railway Corporation and leases from
it the property constructed under Contract No. 4. Stock control over respondent is
held by Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company through ownership of 179,654 shares de-
posited with Central Trust Company, trustee, and 2513 shares held directly.
Important Changes during Year.-Extensions of road put into operation: Broad-
way subway. Times Square to 57th street station; operation begun July 10, 1919,
3.130 miles; 57th st. station to Lexington avenue station; operation begun Sept. 1,
1919, 2.247 miles; northerly track at Lexington avenue station, 60th street line;
operation begun Oct. 30, 1919, 0.10 miles. Culver line, from Avenue X to point of
switch of Brighton line at Coney Island; operation begun May 1, 1920, 2.2 miles.

STREET RAILWAY COMPANIES
281
New York Consolidated Railroad Company
The New York Consolidated R. R. Co. under the agreement of consolidation issued
25 shares of its common stock in exchange for an equal number of shares of the com-
mon stock of the Brooklyn Union Elevated R. R. Co. Bonds were acquired as fol-
lows for employers' liability reserve: U. S. 2d Liberty Loan, 44s, par value,
$83,000; U. S. certificate of indebtedness, $1,740,000. The mortgage investment, A. &
M. Hochman, $4,000, was retired Oct. 1, 1919. Under order issued Aug. 1, 1919, by
Judge Julius M. Mayer, and the approval of the Public Service Commission in Case
No. 2375, joint 2-year, 6 per cent Receiver's certificates of the New York Municipal
Ry. Co. and the New York Consolidated R. R. Co. to the amount of $13,000,000 were
sold at 95. Date of issue, August 1, 1919.
Agreements were made as follows: With the South Brooklyn Ry. Co. Oct. 10,
1919, for the lease of four parcels of land owned by that company the periods of
---
the leases being from 3 to 21 years. With The Brooklyn City R. R. Co., Oct. 31,
1919, relative to the exchange of transfers with the subway at Fourth ave. and 86th
st. With the New York Telephone Co., Dec. 19, 1919, for the installation of public
telephones in subway stations. The fare to Coney Island was reduced to 5 cents,
effective May 1, 1920. No new powers or franchises were acquired.
Fixed Capital Changes during Year
Installed during year, under addition and betterments to other street railway
land, the filling and grading of a 110 ft. strip on Ocean Parkway, $917.12.
Details of Property Retired during Year from Fixed Capital, Dec. 31, 1908, and
Accounts Credited.- Miscellaneous equipment: Removal from Newtown Dock of
four stiff-leg derricks installed in 1898, $5,000. Cost of road and equipment: 63d
street dock property requisitioned by the U. S. Government, $364,700.

NEW YORK MUNICIPAL RAILWAY CORPORATION
LESSOR
Incorporated September 27, 1912. The entire capital stock of this company is
held by the lessee, the New York Consolidated Railroad Company [and the capital
stock of the latter is in turn almost entirely owned by the Brooklyn Rapid Transit
Company.]
Important Changes during Year.- United States Certificates of Indebtedness, 4%%.
par value, $950,000, were acquired in August, 1919. They matured in December, and in
January, 1920, the company reinvested in 4% certificates. A real estate mortgage
for $6,600 was acquired, November 29, 1919. August 1, 1919, Receiver's certificates were
issued jointly with the New York Consolidated maturing in 2 years with interest at
6 per cent, par value,¹ $13,000,000. On October 10, 1919, the South Brooklyn Ry Co.
leased four parcels to the New York Municipal Ry. Corp., the New York Consolidated
R. R. Co. and the City of New York for various terms from 3 to 21 years.
For the extensions of the road put into operation see abstract of the New York
Consolidated Railroad Company, ibid.
No new powers or franchises were acquired.
The 5 per cent discount on this issue, $650,000, is written off as follows: $390,000
was charged to construction under the contract, and 1/24 of $260,000 is charged
off monthly to the lessee company.

NEW YORK RAILWAYS COMPANY
Incorporated December 29, 1911, under sections 9 and 10 of the Stock Corporation
Law, as a reorganization of the Metropolitan Street Railway Company. The New
York Railways Company holds the properties of the following-named companies
under leases or operating agreements:
Bleecker Street and Fulton Ferry Railroad Company.
Broadway and Seventh Avenue Railroad Company.
Christopher and Tenth Streets Railroad Company (agreement).
For George and Eleventh Avenue Railroad Company (agreement).
Forty-Second Street and Grand Street Ferry Railroad Company.
Sixth Avenue Railroad Company.
Thirty-fourth Street Crosstown Railway Company (agreement). *
Twenty-third Street Railway Company.
The respondent holds direct control of these companies through ownership of a
majority of their capital stocks, except for the Christopher and Tenth Streets and
the Sixth Avenue companies. It also has a one-half interest, represented by 500
shares of stock, in the Bridge Operating Company.
Stock control of this company is held by the Interborough Consolidated Corpora-
tion which acquired the majority of its shares June 1, 1915.
Important Changes during Year.- By the terms of the following-named orders of
the U. S. District Court the Receiver ceased to operate certain leased lines - -return-
ing them to their owners:

## p. (#287) ################################################

STREET RAILWAY COMPANIES
287
New York Railways Company
Lines were discontinued temporarily, until further notice, as follows:
Under order of September 12, 1919, efective September 21, the storage battery car
lines known as Avenue "C," Spring and Delancey Street, Madison Street, and the
Sixth Avenue Ferry.
Under other orders of the court changes in operation were made as follows:
**
Effective October 1 (as the result of the separation of the Ninth Avenue Railroad
Co. from the system) operation of the "Sixth-Amsterdam," " Broadway-Columbus."
and Broadway-Amsterdam " avenue lines were discontinued, and in lieu thereof
new routes designated as Sixth Avenue," Broadway-Seventh Avenue," "Colum-
bus-Lenox Avenue" and "53d Street " lines were inaugurated.
64
64
Effective December 1, the operation of the Canal street line was discontinued.
Effective February 1, 1920, service was resumed on the Spring and Delancey street
line from Broadway and Spring street to Delancey and Clinton streets only, and
June 21 it was extended to Lewis street.
Effective February 25, service on the Thirty-fourth street line west of Eleventh
avenue was discontinued.
Effective March 7, operation of through cars on the Seventh Avenue-Brooklyn
line and the Fourteenth street line across the Williamsburg Bridge was discontinued.
Effective March 7, the operation of a new route, designated as the 106th street line,
was inaugurated.
Transfer privileges were changed, also under orders of the court, as follows:
Effective December 1, 1919, transfers were discontinued at Grand street and
Bowery, Grand and Center streets, 34th street and Tenth avenue, and 42d street and
Tenth avenue.
Effective February 1, 1920, transfer interchange with the New York & Harlem R. R.
Co. was inaugurated at Fourth avenue and 34th street, 23d street and 14th street.
Agreements were made between Job E. Hedges, as Receiver of the New York
Railways Company, and other parties as follows:
With the Eighth Avenue Railways Co., July 15, 1919, covering rent of buildings,
tracks, ducts, transfer interchange, and sale of power, material, etc. It is provided
that both parties shall issue transfers which entitle passengers to the same
privileges as if the Eighth Avenue R. R. Co. lines had continued to be operated
by the Receiver. The division of fares as fixed by the arbitrator, Judge Julius
M. Mayer, was 3.7282 cents to the New York Ry. Co. and 3.2718 cents to the Eighth
Avenue (being 53.26% and 46.74% respectively) of the 7-cent joint rate. The result
was a credit of $1 393.37 to the Miscellaneous receipts" of the New York Railways
Co. for the months of August and September, although the receipts from the trans-
fers issued to other lines were credited to transportation revenue on the basis of
"the number lifted." From and after October 1. all transfers to and from the lines
of the Eighth Avenue R. R. Co. were discontinued.
With the Ninth Avenue Railroad Co., September 27, 1919, covering rent of build-
ings, tracks, ducts, transfer interchange, sale of power, material, etc.
With the New York Edison Co., October 22, 1919, covering the delivery to the latter
of alternating current at the rate of 2 cents per kw.hr. as a reserve power service.
With the Interborough Rapid Transit Co., October 23, 1919, covering the purchase
of alternating current at approximately 11,000 volts for redistribution to the New
York Edison Co. at 1½ cents per kw.hr.
With the New York & Harlem R. R., January 31, 1920, covering rent of buildings,
tracks, ducts, etc., transfer interchange and sale of power, material, etc.
With the Collier Service Corporation, May 31, 1920, for street car advertising
privileges, from May 31, 1920, to May 31, 1930, at the rate of $115,000 per annum.
Under the various dates indicated, the Receiver of the New York Railways Com-
pany agreed to the following rates for power furnished by the Interborough Rapid
Transit Company during the year:

Effective August 1, 1919, by and under the terms of an order of the Public Service
Commission in Case No. 2389, a charge of 2¢ was authorized for each transfer de-
manded by a passenger except in the case of transfers required by the terms of a
municipal franchise agreement or consent.
Effective September 15, 1919, by order of the District Court of the United States
for the Southern District dated June 30, 1919, Job E. Hedges, as Receiver of the
New York Railways Company, was directed to terminate agreement dated September
9, 1914, between the City of New York and New York Railways Company providing
for transfers between the Municipal Ferry, operated between Staten Island and
the foot of Whitehall street, and the several lines of the New York Railways Com-
pany described in said agreement so terminated.
Effective October 1, 1919, by and under the terms of an order of the District
Court of the United States for the Southern District of New York, dated November
26, 1919, the Receiver was directed to issue free transfers at certain points along the
route of the Ninth Avenue Railroad Company as required by the terms of municipal
franchise or consent.
Effective October 5, 1919, interchange of transfers with The Brooklyn & North
River Railroad Company was discontinued, due to cessation of the operation of
that company's line.
No new powers or franchises were acquired.

THE NINTH AVENUE RAILROAD COMPANY
Incorporated July 29, 1859.
Principal Stockholders. The twenty largest stockholders, January 5, 1920, and
the number of votes of each were: Anna W. Peter, 2,000; Estate of Sarah Wil-
liams, 537; Alga Law, 457; Mary L. Hays, 374; George Henry Warren, 265; George
Henry Warren, Trustee, 265; Georgia W. Warren, 162; James J. O'Donohue, Jr., 212;

Important Changes during Year.- Pursuant to an order of the United States
District Court, dated August 28, 1919, the lease of the company's property to the
Metropolitan Street Railway Company was cancelled and certain of its property
was surrendered and turned over to the company by the Receiver of the New
York Railways Company, the successor of the Metropolitan Street Railway Com-
pany, at midnight September 30, 1919, when the company resumed operation.
The company on March 25, 1920, sold its property at 1324-8 Amsterdam avenue,
book value $9,929.48, for $50,000.00 accepting from the Hermes Amusement Co. in
part payment therefor a 6% mortgage for $35,000.00, due March 25, 1925.
No new
powers or franchises acquired.

equipment, $834.73; total, $1,128.90.

PELHAM PARK AND CITY ISLAND RAILWAY COMPANY, INC.
Incorporated May 10, 1913, under sections 9 and 10 of the State Corporation Law,
as a reorganized company; successor to the City Island R. R. Co. and the
Pelham Park R. R. Co. Control of the company was acquired July 9, 1914,
by the Third Avenue Railway Co, through the purchase of all its stock.
Important Changes during Year.- No cars were operated after August 9, 1919,
at 12:01 a. m. The six leased cars were returned to their owners. No new
powers or franchises were acquired.

RICHMOND LIGHT AND RAILROAD COMPANY
Incorporated [as an electric lighting company] August 1, 1902, under the Trans-
portation Corporations Law, chapter 566, Laws of 1890, as amended. On August
18, 1902, an amended certificate was filed enabling the company to build and
operate an electric railroad.
The twenty principal stockholders and the number of votes to which they
were entitled were: Leslie M. McCrum, 4,400; E. J. Boyd, 4,000; W. B. Cardoza,
4,000; Thos. F. Ryan, 2,332; George B. M. Harvey, 1,971; N. D. Putnam, 1,836;
Austin B. Fletcher, 1,431; Lewis B. Franklin, 719; J. K. Rice, Jr. & Co., 550;
Sidney Aronstein, 900; Lehman Bros., 500; Brown Bros. & Co., 349; Albert C.
Burrage, 312; Orviss Bros. & Co., 200; Chandler Bros. & Co., 247; A. J. Hemphill,
201; Levi Holbrook, 200; W. C. Bradley, 200; H. B. Rodeson, 172; Harold Stanley,
200.
Important Changes during Year.- J. J. Kuhn was appointed Federal Receiver and
began operating the road April 28, 1920. Rates of fare were changed May 5, 1920,
as follows: Cash fares from 5¢ to 84, regular tickets from 5 to 7½c, school tickets
from 4 to 34. The company purchased cars for $67,500.00, of which $61,000.00 was
in the form of equipment obligations. No new powers or franchises were acquired.

Incorporated January 21, 1853. The twenty principal stockholders and the

Important Changes during Year.- Agreements were made as follows: [January 31,
1920, with the New York and Harlem Railroad Company for the sale of power to
that company at cost on a car mileage basis]; March 15, 1920, with the same com-
pany for its use of part of the car house of the Second Ave. R. R. Co., consideration
$3,000 per month.
Agreement with New York Rys. Co. whereby transfers issued by that company's
Eighth avenue line and honored by the 59th street line of Belt Line Ry. Corp.
would be accepted by the Second Ave. R. R. Co., was discontinued September 30,
1919, by the Eighth Avenue R. R. Co.
There was default in payment of principal and interest on Receiver's Certificates
issued October 1, 1918, and due October 1, 1920. On February 5, 1920, Charles E.
Chalmers was appointed Receiver, succeeding Mr. Kalbach, who died February 2,
1920.
No new powers or franchises were acquired.

SOUTH BROOKLYN RAILWAY COMPANY
LESSEE OF PROSPECT PARK AND CONEY ISLAND RAILROAD COMPANY AND IT LEASED LINES
Incorporated January 13, 1900.
The control of this company was acquired August 31, 1902, by the Brooklyn Rapid
Transit Company which owns all its stock except the directors' shares.
Important Changes during Year.- Investments were changed by the acquisition
of $5,500 U. S. 2nd Liberty Loan 44% bonds and the retirement of the $1,000 O'Reilly
mortgage. This company leased to the New York Municipal Railway Co., the New
York Consolidated Railroad Co. and the City of New York the 36th street repair
shops for a period of twenty-one years; the 39th street shop for a period of ten
years, and also two other parcels for periods of ten and three years respectively.
For the contract with the Post Office Department see the abstract of the Brooklyn
City Railroad Co. A contract for the purchase of six safety single truck cars was
made November 29, 1918. No new powers or franchises were acquired.

STATEN ISLAND MIDLAND RAILWAY COMPANY
Incorporated January 16, 1907. The stockholders and the number of votes to
which they were entitled were as follows: Estate of H. H. Rogers, 9,984; W. E.
Benjamin, 1; W. R. Coe, 1; E. C. Lackland, Jr., 1; Geo. H. Church, 1; Jno. T.

Important Changes during Year.- Jacob Brenner was appointed Receiver and
began operation of the property January 19, 1920. Steel cars Nos. 300 to 331 were
sold to the Richmond Lt. & R. R. Co., which assumed the obligation to pay the
balance of the obligation therefor, viz., $81,000. Operations were discontinued Janu-
ary 18, 1920, and the trackage of the Richmond Light & R. R. Co. surrendered; on
May 21, operation was resumed on the Concord line only. No new franchises or
powers were acquired.

THIRD AVENUE BRIDGE COMPANY
LESSORI
Incorporated May 29, 1909. The entire capital stock of the respondent was ac-
quired by the Third Avenue Railway Company on March 25, 1915.
Important Changes during Year:- The agreement under which the Forty-Second
Street, Manhattanville & St. Nicholas Avenue R. R. Co. operated cars over the
franchise and tracks of this company was terminated July 3, 1919, and rental for
the lease was not received after November 1, 1919.
[The Board of Estimate of the City of New York in a resolution adopted on
January 30, 1920, declared forfeited the contract which granted franchise to this
company to operate from Third Avenue, 59th street and 60th street across the
Queensboro Bridge.]
In July and December, 1919, the company bought for investment of its sinking
fund, $42,000, par value, of the Third Avenue Ry. Co. 1st and ref. 4% mortgage bonds,
due Jan. 1, 1960, at a cost of $23,412.40. No new powers or franchises were acquired.
Fixed Capital Changes during Year:- Nore.
Corporate Surplus.- Credit balance at beginning of year, $6,000; loss for year,
$$26.80. Surplus, June 30, 1920, $5,173.20.
Officers.- President, S. W. Huff; Vice-President, L. Sutherland; Secretary, W. C.
Burrows; Assistant Secretary, J. Maroney; Treasurer, A. D. Sage; Assistant
Treasurer, F. Kessel; Auditor, B. G. Steinetz; Assistant Auditor, W. Farrington;
General Counsel, A. T. Davison.
Directors.-E. M. Burghard, G. W. Davison, S. W. Huff, A. Iselin, Jr., E. A.
Manice, J. A. Platten, W. E. Roosevelt, J. H. Seaman.
Main Business Office- 2396 Third Avenue, New York City.
Report verified by A. D. Sage, Treasurer, September 30, 1920.
THIRD AVENUE RAILWAY COMPANY
Incorporated April 21, 1910, under sections 9 and 10 of the Stock Corporations
Law, as a reorganization of The Third Avenue Railroad Company.
Respondent controls the following corporations through ownership of a majority
of their capital stock:
Belt Line Railway Corporation..
The Dry Dock East Broadway and Battery Railroad Company.
The Forty-second Street, Manhattanville and St. Nicholas Avenue Railway Com-
pany.
Kingsbridge Railway Company.

FIRST DISTRICT
PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION
Third Avenue Railway Company
Mid-Crosstown Railway Company, Inc.

The Southern Boulevard Railroad Company.
Third Avenue Bridge Company.
Union Railway Company of New York City.
The Westchester Electric Railroad Company.
The Yonkers Railroad Company.
Hastings Railway Company, Inc.
New York City Interborough Railway Company.
It also controls the Bronx Traction Company indirectly through its control of
the Union Railway Company of New York City. Together with other companies it
has joint control of the Brooklyn and North River Railroad Company.
The twenty principal stockholders and voting bond holders and the number of
votes to which they were entitled on November 11, 1919 were: N. Y. Life Ins. Co.,
15,900; A. Iselin Co., 14,340; Equitable Life Ins. Co., 14,000; Metropolitan Life Ins.
Co., 6,625; Harvard College, 5,550; Roosevelt & Son, 5,400; James Ann, 5,000; George
Ehret, 2,880; Clark, Childs & Co., 2,095; Christopher Gallagher, 2,000; L. F. Straus,
1,800; H. F. Clews, 1,619; Dominick & Dominick, 1,525; Lewis A. Galrung, 1,200;
Mrs. A. N. Harkness, 900; G. H. Kindall, 700; W. F. Armstrong, 700; Post & Flagg,
634; W. H. Douglas, 600; G. A. Dickley, 600.
Voting rights are also attached to adjustment 5 per cent income bonds. Until
interest at 5 per cent per annum, together with accumulations have been paid for
five consecutive years, registered wners have one vote for each $100 par value of
bonds.
Important Changes during Year.- Purchased: New York City Interborough Rail-
way Company, common stock par value $10,000 for $300; City of New York corporate
stock, $5,000 at par; 2d Liberty loan bonds, $187,950 at par; 3d Liberty loan bonds,
$133,250 at par; 4th Liberty loan bonds, $263,050 at par; and Victory notes, $8,350 at
par. Subscribed for $2,000 stock of Hastings Railway Co., Inc. at par. Brooklyn
and North River stock, par and book value, $25,000, reduced in book value to $1.00.
There was a decrease of .046 in mileage due to removal of track from Ninth
avenue between 218th and 220th street.
No additional powers or franchises were acquired.

LESSOR
Incorporated [March 18, 1896] as a consolidation of two companies. The New
York Railways Company owns all the capital stock of this company, which was
acquired December 30, 1911, by purchase and reorganization of the Metropolitan
Street Railway Co.; it is also the operator of the property.

TWENTY-THIRD STREET RAILWAY COMPANY
LESSOR
Incorporated June 29, 1872; controlled by the New York Railways Co., which
holds 5,066 out of 6,000 shares of capital stock outstanding. The New York Railways
Co. as lessee has not paid that part of the rental consisting of 18% dividends
the capital stock ($108,000 per annum) since February 1, 1919, and the
Receiver of that company to June 30, 1920, had neither affirmed nor disaffirmed the
lease.
on
Important Changes during Year.- The trustee of the sinking fund acquired $6,000,
par value, improvement and refunding mortgage 5% bonds of this company at a
cost of $3,508.89. No new powers or franchises were acquired. Track in the Twenty-
third Street yard was removed to the extent of 1,450 feet.
Fixed Capital Changes during Year.- None.
Corporate Surplus.- Credit balance at beginning of year, $120,577.06; net corporate
income of year, $110,816.97. Surplus, June 30, 1920, $231,394.03. This includes rental
accrued but unpaid, $155,250, which was not included in the balance sheet.

UNION RAILWAY COMPANY OF NEW YORK CITY
Incorporated April 20, 1892. [In January, 1899, control of the company through
acquisition of its capital stock was obtained by the Third Avenue Railroad Company,
which was succeeded, January 1, 1912, pursuant to a plan of re-organization, by
the Third Avenue Railway Company.]
The company controls the Bronx Traction Company through entire stock owner-
ship.
Important Changes during Year.- The length of track operated was decreased
.255 miles due to removal of connecting curves and sidings. Investments were as
follows: Advances to Bronx Traction Co. were increased over 1919 by $15,402.94.
Purchased Third Avenue Railway Co. 1st refunding 4% mortgage bonds, par value
$27,500, actual money cost and amount at which carried on books, $14,675.54. No
new power or franchises were acquired.

THE VAN BRUNT STREET AND ERIE BASIN RAILROAD COMPANY
Incorporated February 15, 1861.

28, 1920, were as follows: John F. Murphy, Wyllys Terry, Dennis W. Sullivan,
and John A. Foulds as Trustees, 13,381; Joseph C. Hecker, Jr., 2,410; Andrew D.
Parker, 2,278; Martin J. Burns, 668; Emma C. Taylor, 666; Esther C. M. McCullough,

Edward M. De Castro, 1 each.
Important Changes during Year.- Transfers to and from the Hamilton Avenue

THE WESTCHESTER ELECTRIC RAILROAD COMPANY
Incorporated March 27, 1891. [In January, 1899, control of the company was
acquired through acquisition of its capital stock by the Third Avenue Railroad
Company, which was succeeded, January 1, 1912, pursuant to plan of re-organization,
by the Third Avenue Railway Company.]
Important Changes during Year. The length of the company's entire track was
increased by 0.403 miles, of which 0.115 was in New York City on Webster avenue
to crossover at 198th street; 0.287 miles in Mt. Vernon on North Third avenue from
East 1st street to Oakley avenue; 0.001 miles in Eastchester due to remeasurement of
White Plains road from Mill road to Scarsdale line. Road was put into operation
as follows: from North 4th avenue and 1st street in Mount Vernon to White
Plains road and Scarsdale line, in Eastchester, known as the Bronxville line;
length of road, 4.964 miles and of track 5.40 miles; operation was begun February
26, 1920. No new powers or franchises were acquired.

NOTE. Where track is owned jointly, only the proportionate share of each company is included
under each. Where track is operated under a joint franchise, it is assigned to the owning company
if definitely known; otherwise it is divided between the participating companies. Track owned
under franchise with right of reversion in the city is classed as "owned." Mileage under trackage
rights that involve duplicate use of tracks, is, of course, not included under the company exercising
such rights.
Returns of trackage have often been slightly inaccurate and inconsistent. Prior to the 1917
tabulation, the published figures were those returned for the close of the year. In the 1917 to
1919 tables, they were the 1916 figures as modified by returns of new or removed track. In the
1920 tabulation, the earlier method has been resumed; but the north pair of "surface" tracks
on the Williamsburg bridge has been apportioned between the N. Y. Rys. and the Dry Dock on
the basis of tolls paid, instead of assigning them entirely to the former as being the company with
which the city directly entered into the bridge privilege agreement. By reason of these changes,
the 1920 mileage of some surface companies is not comparable with previously published figures,
Includes underground, 0.290 miles of first track and 0.290 miles of second.

1 Points where transfer (either free or 2-cent) slips are used to effect a change of cars.
locality basis involves no duplication except in the case of exchange of transfers between companies,
in which case each company counts each locality. With a few exceptions, such inter-company
transfers occur only among associated companies. The second basis used relates to the viewpoint
of the car line by which the transfer stip is issued and counts each point where two or more lines
exchange transfers. Data entered above are compiled from tariff reports instead of from the
annual returns of the companies. Returns for transfer points classified with reference to whether
the transfer is made to other lines of the same company, to lines of associated companies, or to
those of companies not associated, are presented in Table VIII as returned by the companies for
individual lines. With one exception (see note 2) change from one line to another of rapid transit
companies is made without transfer slips. At 149th street and Third avenue change between
subway and elevated divisions of the Interborough is made by transfer slip. See last sentence
of note 1. 3-10 Includes post-office revenue: $3,862.50; $1,279.80; $8,899.24; 6 $3,568.16;

NOTE. Because of the routing of trains of one line over sections of track of other lines, station
ticket sales cannot in all cases be definitely classed or equitably apportioned by routes. Tickets
sold on branches jointly operated by trains of the Subway Division and Manhattan Railway
Division are shared in proportion to ton-mileage of trains operated over the respective branches.

1 The separation between north and southbound traffic is as reported by the company. There
is a passageway between the two platforms.
2 The Grand Central stations under Contracts 1 and 3 are separate but connected by passages,
so that a passenger can purchase a ticket at one, but board a train at the other.
Leads to same train platforms as the Mott Avenue station of the West Farms branch.

NOTE.- Passengers between New York and New Jersey via the Christopher street tunnels
pay a supplementary two-cent fare. All the traffic represented by the italic figures is therefore
included in the figures printed in roman type, but not always in the figure just above, since the
number of two-cent tickets sold on the Sixth Avenue branch represents not only southbound
passengers but also some northbound. In April, 1920, the company changed its method of
reporting ticket sales. Theretofore the distribution among stations was estimated; thereafter
the company reported actual traffic by stations.

figure of Div. A, in some cases because the schedule on which the former is based contains the num-

schedule on which Div. A is based merely gives the number working on the last day of the year.

are unexplained.

Depreciation.- Inasmuch as the definition of expenses, in the Uniform System of Accounts
prescribed by this Commission includes capital consumed in operation, the accounting order pro-
vides for a charge to operating expenses for the estimated depreciation accrued during each month,
in addition to actual disbursements for repairs. Where a company has failed to make a charge for
depreciation, a reference mark (*) has been placed against the totals of the maintenance groups and
the grand total of operating expenses. Credit entries (Cr) indicate that the expenditures for main-
tenance have exceeded the estimated depreciation (including maintenance), the excess being drawn
from (or charged against) the depreciation reserve. 1 Other than cleaning and sanding track,
and removal of snow, ice and sand. 2 Class A classification is used by corporations with annual
operating revenues of more than $500,000, and also by corporations affiliated with such Class A
corporations, even though with operating revenues under $500,000; Class B classification is used by
corporations with annual operating revenues of more than $100,000 but not more than $500,000,

NOTE. When the lessee pays the interest on the bonded debt of the lessor direct to the holders
of the bonds, the amount of such accruals is entered in this table as a part of the rental and also
as an income deduction, whether or not it appears on the books of the lessor. When the corporate
expenses of the lessor company are paid wholly or in part by the lessee, in addition to a fixed rental,
the amount of such expenses, as reported by the lessee, is entered in this table as part of the rent

• Consolidated balance sheet of Company and Receiver.
7 Includes cost of property, etc., acquired under terms of re-organization plan and agreement of
sale of the Metropolitan Street Railway $74,671,206.21 less $152,353.25 salvage from property
retired. These amounts have not been distributed among the accounts affected. The former
amount, in addition to fixed capital, includes miscellaneous investments. Also includes property
of Central Crosstown R. R. Co. ($250,000 additional representing amount of mortgage thereon)
acquired August 14, 1918.
The New York Railways during 1920 relinquished the leases under which it held properties of
the Eighth Avenue, Ninth Avenue and New York & Harlem, the properties thus reverting to the
owners with additions and improvements thereon. The Eighth and Ninth Avenue companies by
methods not in accordance with the Uniform System of Accounts have arrived at valuations of the
transferred property, although title to certain portions of it has not been determined by the courts.
These valuations have been taken into their fixed capital accounts, as shown in the 1920 reports,
surplus being correspondingly increased. The New York Railways, however, fails to show this
transfer on its books. In view of the unsettled questions both as to proper book value and as to
title of the property involved, Fixed capital is tabulated here as of June 30, 1919, plus net of additions
and withdrawals. For the Eighth and Ninth Avenue companies this method of tabulation results
in a concurrent reduction in Surplus of $4,798,602.05 and $2,807,262.05 respectively, the amounts
reported as transferred from New York Railways; for the New York & Harlem & New York Rail-
ways, it agrees with 1920 reports.
Reported on liability side as contingent account and not deducted by the company from Fixed
capital. See note 7 on third double page of § 4, Div. B of this table.

## p. (#542) ################################################

542
PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION FIRST DISTRICT
CITY ISLAND MOTOR BUS COMPANY, INC.
Organized April 27, 1916, under the laws of 1854.
Stockholders and members.- Total number, 9; all residing in the state of New
York.
Important Changes during the Year.- New interests took over the corporate
stock, and an entirely new board of directors was elected October 1, 1919.
Balance Sheet as of June 30, 1920.- Assets: Revaluation of equipment, net
investments, $649.52; cash, $633.83; miscellaneous accounts receivable, $342.69;
other current assets, deposit City of New York, $500; deferred assets, franchise
$1,000 and organization expense $898.02; total $4,024.06. Liabilities: Capital stock,
common outstanding, $5,800; loans and notes payable, $3,029.23; accounts payable,
$6,785.26; due City of New York for taxes on transportation, $16,018.33; deficit,
$27,608.76; total, $4,024.06.
1
Property Account.- Equipment: Balance at beginning of year, $3,916.41; with-
drawn, $3,266.89; balance at close of year, 2 $649.52.
Description of Vehicles in Service at Close of Year.- Gasoline motor omnibuses,
passenger service: 2 16-passenger Reos, weight each 3,750 lbs.; 1 12-passenger
Chalmers, weight 2,500 lbs. Service cars: 1 Ford runabout, 1913 model.
Description of Vehicles Withdrawn within the Year.- Gasoline motor omnibuses,
passenger service: 1 16-passenger Reo, weight 2,750 lbs., original cost $740.55,
charged to reserve.
Capital Stock.- Common, par value, per share, $100; authorized by articles of
incorporation, $10,000; amount actually issued and outstanding to close of year,
$5,800, for cash, $2,000 and property, $3,800.
INCOME STATEMENT

Employees and Wages.- Number of officers and employees on last pay-roll of
June: Officers, 2; clerks, 1; drivers,3 1; checkers, 3; superintendents, 1. Aggre-
gate compensation for year: Officers, $3,550; clerks, $885; drivers, $2,672.12;
checkers, $4,276.08; superintendents, $2,651; mechanics, $723.84; total number on
pay-roll, 8; total compensation for year, $14,758.04.
Traffic Statistics.- Route 7.5 miles from West Farms station to Belden Point,
City Island, via Boston Road, White Plains ave., Pelham Parkway and City
Island ave. Number of bus trips, 42,011. Passengers carried distributed by fares
as follows: Number of passengers at 15 cents, 29,720, revenue $4,458; at 20 cents,
503,628, revenue $100,725.60. Total number of passengers, 533,348, total amount of
revenue, $105,183.60.
Accidents: 1 automobile accident, in which 4 persons were injured. No other
accidents occurred during the year.
Officers.- President, William Clark; Vice-President, Fritz H. Bose; Treasurer,
Oliver Dyson; Secretary, Eric Jungberg.
Directors. William Clark, Fritz H. Bose, Oliver Dyson, Eric Jungberg, George
Heath, Henry Kaplan, Mathes Eihenlaub, Jack H. Holmquist, Joseph Coppola.
Main Business Office.- 1044 East Tremont Ave. corner Boston Road, New York
City.
Report verified by William Clark, President, October 13, 1920.
FIFTH AVENUE COACH COMPANY
Incorporated July 25, 1896. Control of respondent was acquired on November
30, 1899, by the New York Transportation Company, which owns all of the out-
standing stock.
Important Changes during the Year.- Government and railroad bonds were
acquired at a cost of $856,935.88, par value, $1,029,000; and railroad bonds were sold
for $235,507.50; par value, $412.000; cost, $345,561.25; loss, $110,053.75; for details see
schedule Miscellaneous Investments.
The agreement dated December 23, 1915, whereby the Company granted and leased
to the New York Transportation Company exclusive advertising privileges in the
buses, was renewed on the same terms and conditions as per agreement dated
June 30, 1919, for a further period of three years, i. e. to June 30, 1922.
No new powers or franchises were acquired.
3 Decrease in the number of drivers due to fact that company leased buses and
the owners supplied the drivers to operate them.
The wages of 2 mechanics on the payroll for the quarter ending Sept. 1919,
none being employed for the remainder of the year.

Depreciation or Amortization. For provisions of the Uniform System of Accounts
for street railways as to depreciation, see page 238. Under date of January 28, 1916,
the company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation effective July 1, 1915,
estimating an amount equal to 6.4 cents per bus mile to be sufficient and neces-
sary to cover such wear and tear, obsolescence and inadequacy as may occur on all
equipment. Under the above rule the company charged under maintenance of plant
and equipment, $186,304.09 to Depreciation of equipment, $10,018.55 to Depreciation of
shop tools and machinery (10 per cent per annum) and $16,685.59 to Depreciation of
buildings (2 per cent of cost per annum).
The company charged to Miscellaneous stage service expenses and concurrently
credited to the reserve for depreciation of uniforms $11,511.04, representing propor-
tionate amount of cost of overcoats furnished drivers and uniforms furnished to
starters and inspectors.
Amortization: The company charged $4.804.08 to Miscellaneous rent deductions;
$3,764.40 represents a proportionate amount of the cost of reconstructing the 88th
street garage and $1,039.68 represents a proportionate amount of the cost of im-
provements made on the building Nos. 4-20 East 102nd street, in order to amortize
the full cost of the former at the expiration of the lease, December 31, 1920, and
of the latter, June 30, 1934.

## p. (#556) ################################################

556
PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION - FIRST DISTRICT
Fifth Avenue Coach Company
Officers.- President, John A. Ritchie; Secretary and Auditor, Samuel E. Morrow;
Assistant Secretary, Thomas A. Newton; Treasurer, George L. Willems; General
Counsel, William II. Page; Claim Agent, George Carson; General Manager, George
A. Green.
Directors.- Edward J. Berwind, Philip T. Dodge, Fred L. Lavanburg, W. Leon
Pepperman, John A. Ritchie, Henry Sanderson, Frank Hedley, Herbert H. Vree-
land, Edmond E. Wise.
Main Business Office.-10 East 102nd street, New York City.
Report verified by John A. Ritchie, President, September 30, 1920.
THE NEW YORK TRANSFER COMPANY
Organized as a joint stock association November 1, 1870. The company's busi-
ness is transferring baggage from residences and hotels to railroad and steamboat
terminals and vice versa.
Stockholders and Members.- Total number of stockholders, June 30, 1920, 146;
of whom 45 reside in New York State. Two directors, S. W. F. Draper and
G. W. Stetson held proxies jointly in their names for a majority of the stock.
for voting at shareholders' meeting; the proxies were not used.
June 30,

## p. (#560) ################################################

560
PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION - FIRST DISTRICT
The New York Transfer Company
Officers.- President, S. W. F. Draper; Vice-President, Wm. H. Woolverton; Secre-
tary and Treasurer, F. K. Waller.
Directors.-S. W. F. Draper, W. G. Besler, John S. Durand, F. W. Allen, Alfred
Hayes, William Thompson, George L. Connor, Wm. H. Woolverton.
Main Business Office.-1354 Broadway, New York City.
Report verified by F. K. Waller, Treasurer, October 5, 1920.
WESTCOTT EXPRESS COMPANY
History. Formed by consolidation of Westcott Express Company (original com-
pany of same name) and the Consolidated Transfer Company under Section 8 of the
Business Corporations Law; certificate of incorporation filed with Secretary of
State, Nov. 28, 1894. Respondent's business is the transfer of baggage to and from
residences and railroad terminals. The American Express Company acquired con-
trol over the respondent on March 31, 1903, by the purchase of a majority of its
capital stock.
Important Changes during Year.-Agreements were discontinued as follows:
With the D., L. & W. R. R., Jan. 31, 1919, consideration, $1,800; with N. Y. Central
R. R., Dec. 1, 1919, consideration, $6,000; with Troy Union Ry., Dec. 1, 1919, con-
sideration, $600. No additional powers or franchises were acquired.

1919

NOTE. Companies omitted under any apparatus do not report any. Only the important kinds of apparatus for which returns are made are tabulated. Columns
for installations and withdrawals show capacity, with number of units in parentheses.
1 Data for boilers and engines of the Bronx Gas & Electric, while applicable to both gas works and electric power plant, are all shown in the corresponding
table for power-plant apparatus (XV) and not entered here.

## p. (#693) ################################################

GAS COMPANIES AND ELECTRICAL COMPANIES
693
NOTE.- A majority of the companies billed consumers for gas at a price in excess of the statutory maximum, but such action as a rule was taken pursuant to
an injunction of the court which required the excess collected to be deposited with the court pending trial and judgment. The rates charged by the different com-
panies in 1920 were as follows:
A-On July 1 increased from 80c. to $1.00 and August 6 to $1.20.
B
July 1 increased from 80c. to $1.00 and December 1 to $1.10.
C-November 19 increased from $1.00 to $1.25 plus a monthly service charge of 75c. (2)¢. per day).
D-March 1 increased from 80c. to 97c. and December 1 to $1.10.
E-December 1 increased from $1.00 (Newtown) and 80c. (Flatbush) to $1.10.
October 23 increased from 95c. to $1.50. Prior to March 29, 1920, bonds of par value equal to the excess collected over the 80c. statutory rate were de-
posited with the Empire Trust Company by order of the State Supreme Court. These deposits were increased by $16,843.68 in 1920.
F-
April 1 increased from $1.10 to $1.15 and August 1 to $1.40 by permission of Public Service Commission. The Supreme Court pronounced the 80c. rate
G
confiscatory in 1918, no deposit or bond being required for the excess.
H-The company collected $1.50 throughout the year, being required to furnish Court with $50,000 bond for return to its gas consumers of the excess over
the $1.00 statutory rate or other reasonable rate which may later be established.
* In addition the following amounts were collected in excess of statutory rates, impounded and not taken into revenue: Consolidated Gas, $2,796,728.08; New
Amsterdam, $342,039.18; Central Union, $233,944.70; Standard, $216,154.21; N. Y. Mutual, $165,436.05 (transferred to Surplus); Northern Union, $166,568.63;
N. Y. & Queens Gas, $16,332.25; total, Consolidated system, $3,937,203.10; Brooklyn Union, $1,956,854.93; Newtown, $3,183.21; Flatbush, $13,441.78; total
Brooklyn Union system, $1,973,479.92; grand total, $5,910,683.02.
""cost" plus 10 per cent commission, the cost therein being credited to operating expenses (Table
XXXIV) while the 10 per cent commission on Others' gas is tabulated here under Other miscellaneous revenues.
Does not include receipt under "agency agreement,"
2 Consists of $8,228,499.28, representing actual cost of labor, materials and other expenses, reported under sales to other gas companies, and $1,198,230.60,
representing return on investment, reported under joint gas rent revenue.

3 Includes $12.80 for private street lamps.
Figures for the Westchester Lighting operations are given for the purposes of statistical completeness, but are not included in the totals.
Includes $3,953.52, commercial heat and power.
Includes $1,195,673.39 at 95c. and $348,517.80 at $1.54.
7 Includes $270, street lighting from individuals and companies.
8 Includes $446.66, commercial heat and power.
'Gas from municipal buildings has been billed N. Y. City at the regular consumers' rate of $1.50 per M cu. ft., but has not been paid.

The accrued amortization (depreciation) has, in a few instances, been determined or approved
by this Commission. The amounts here tabulated represent, in addition to the balances accumu-
lated in the prescribed account, those balances which various companies show on the liabilities side
of the balance sheet under "Accrued amortization of capital," "Renewals and replacements
plant withdrawn from service," "Contingency," etc., which together are similar in character
(although apparently more comprehensive than the prescribed account with its prescribed elements)
and which also contain accumulations made through the prescribed accounts in former years.
For information as to any individual company's present procedure, see Depreciation in that com-
pany's abstract in this volume.
