## p. (#763) ################################################

NOTE REGARDING DEPRECIATION ACCOUNTS
The Uniform System of Accounts prescribed by the Commission for electrical
corporations, as well as that for gas corporations, provides that operating
expenses shall include capital consumed, defined as the "amount estimated to be
necessary to cover such wear and tear and obsolescence and inadequacy as have
accrued" during the fiscal period. Until otherwise ordered, such estimated deteri-
oration is based on a rule determined by the accounting corporation and filed with
the Commission. From the total estimated deterioration during the period there
is deducted the actual amount expended for repairs and the remainder is charged
to the expense account (841) General Amortization and concurrently credited to the
balance sheet account (374) Accrued Amortization of Capital, the text of which is
reprinted below. Reasons for the preference of the term "amortization" over the
more common " depreciation" are stated in the following explanatory note (under
par. 30):
•
By amortization of any charge or credit is meant its gradual extinction.
The word is broader than the word 'depreciation,' since the latter is restricted
ordinarily to tangible property. The word depreciation' also imports more of the
idea of fluctuating value and is complicated somewhat with the question of cost
of replacement at market prices. Because it is considered unnecessary in con-
nection with the gradual consumption or expiration of life of capital to consider
the question of cost of replacement until the replacement is actually made, at which
time the cost of replacement is duly charged to the appropriate account, and
because provision is necessary for the gradual extinction of certain charges (such
as those for some kinds of capital, those for extraordinary casualties, for discount
on debt, etc.), to which the term ' depreciation' does not well apply, it is con-
sidered advisable to use the term amortization' in connection with the extinc-
tion of such charges, and of certain correspondng credits, such as premiums on
debt outstanding."
64
E842. GENERAL AMORTIZATION - ELECTRIC
Charge to this account month by month the amount estimated to be necessary to
cover such wear and tear and obsolescence and inadequacy as have accrued during
the month in. the tangible electric capital of the corporation, such portion of the
life of intangible fixed capital as has expired or been consumed during the month.
and the amount estimated to be necessary to provide a reserve to cover the cost of
property destroyed by extraordinary casualties; less the amounts charged for that
month to the various repair accounts in Electric Operating Expenses. The amount
charged (or credited) to this account shall be concurrently credited (or charged)
to the reserve account No. 374, "Accrued Amortization of Capital." (See page 27.)
Note A.- Until otherwise ordered, the amount estimated to be necessary to
cover such wear and tear and obsolescence and inadequacy as have accrued during
any month shall be based on a rule determined by the accounting corporation.
Such rule may be derived from a consideration of the said corporation's history
and experience during the preceding five years, and the accrual may be on the
basis of kilowatt-hours sold. Amortization of intangible capital shall likewise be
based on rule. Whatever may be its basis, such rules and a sworn statement of
the facts and expert opinions and estimates upon which they are based shall be
filed with the Public Service Commission on or before January 1, 1909; each amend-
ment of any such rule and a sworn statement of the facts and expert opinions
and estimates upon which such amendment is based shall be filed with the Public
Service Commission before it is used by the accounting corporations, and shall
show the date when it is to be effective. Such rules and statements shall be filed
upon sheets 8% inches by 11 inches or 9½ inches by 12 inches and shall be entitled
Rule of the (here naming the accounting corporation) concerning Amortization
of Capital."
Note B. When any capital is retired from service, the amount (estimated if not
known) originally charged to a capital account in respect thereof shall be cred-
ited to such capital account, and (except as provided in account No. E100,"Fixed
Capital, December 31, 1908,") the original money cost of such capital, less salvage.
if any, shall be charged to the reserve account. "Accrued Amortization of Capital."
any necessary adjusting entry being made in the proper account under "Corporate
Surplus or Deficit." Where capital is substantially continuous and can not be
satisfactorily individualized, it shall be kept in efficient operating condition through
repair, and the renewals and replacements of parts thereof shall be considered
repairs. In the case of buildings, towers, bridges, trestles, and other separate
structures capable of being readily individualized, charges to this account must be
sufficient to provide (in respect of such capital and except as provided in account
No. E100,"Fixed Capital, December 31, 1908,") in the account "Accrued Amortiza-
[763]


## p. (#764) ################################################

764
PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION FIRST DISTRICT
tion of Capital" by the time such structures go out of service a reserve equal to
the original money cost thereof, less salvage, to which account such original cost,
less salvage, shall be charged.
374.
ACCRUED AMORTIZATION OF CAPITAL
Credit to this account such amounts as are charged from time to time to
"Operating Expenses," or other accounts to cover depreciation of plant and equip-
ment, and other amortization of capital. When any capital is retired from service,
the original money cost thereof (estimated if not known, and where estimated,
that fact and the facts upon which the estimate is based shall be stated in the
entry), less salvage, shall (except as provided in account No. E100,"Fixed Capital,
December 31, 1908") be charged to this account. The amount originally entered
or contained in the charges to any capital account in respect of such capital so
going out of service shall be credited to such capital account, and any necessary
adjusting entry made to the appropriate sub-account under the account
"Cor-
porate Surplus or Deficit."
(G100.) FIXED CAPITAL, DECEMBER 31, 1908
Charge to this account all the Fixed Capital of the accounting person or cor-
poration devoted to electric operations as such capital stood at the close of
December 31, 1908. Such charges shall be made at the figures at which such capital
was carried on the books of the said person or corporation on that date. When
any capital included in such account is retired from service, the amount at which
it is charged therein shall be credited to this account; the amount of deprecia-
tion or other amortization thereon applicable to the period subsequent to Decem-
ber 31, 1908, shall be charged to the account (No. 374), “Accrued Amortization of
Capital," proper account shall be taken of any salvage, and the remainder of the
amount originally charged to capital shall be concurrently charged to the Cor-
porate Surplus or Deficit account (or equivalent account carried on the books on
December 31, 1908), unless there was carried on the books at that date a reserve
to cover retirement of capital from service, in which case the said concurrent
charge shall be made to such retirement reserve account. If the amount at which
the said capital in service on December 31, 1908, and subsequently retired from
service, was included in the said account is not disclosed on the books and records
of the accounting person or corporation and is not within the knowledge of such
person or corporation or officers or other employees thereof, it shall be estimated,
and such estimated amount shall be treated as is above directed for the actual
amount, the fact of estimation being stated in the entry.
The text of the account referred to is as follows:

1 This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e. amortization of the cost of
perishable property (see Depreciation), page 771; but it reports on the liabilities
side of the balance sheet certain reserves (Renewals and replacements: Contingency
reserve) that contain accumulations made in former years through the prescribed
account. As no segregation is made of such accumulations, the entire credit
balances are necessarily deducted from fixed capital or cost of property in the
same manner as accumulations under the prescribed account. "Accrued amortiza-
tion of capital," representing the expired life or amortized portion of fixed capital
outlay.
2 [Represents advances for expenditures for account of East River Gas Co. of

Gas oil used, 3,782,826 gallons; cost, $270,924.60; oil tar produced, 548,673 gallons;
value thereof, $23,063.20; charged by New Amsterdam Gas Co. based on invoice cost
of oil used by them for respondent's account, $270,924.60, less allowance for value
of residuals produced, $23,769.52, plus 10%, $24,715.51; total, $271,870.59 charged to
coal gas enricher.

Operating Expenses. For details, see Table XXXIII.
Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of March 1, 1909,
this company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides for
a charge to "General amortization gas" and concurrent credit to "Accrued
amortization of capital" of 9.5 cents per M cu. ft. manufactured. The company's
procedure, however, is not in accordance either with this rule or with the require-
ments of the accounting order. Instead it has included in operating expenses
without authority from the Commission the item "Renewals and Replacements -
plant withdrawn from service," charging thereto in 1920, 0.5 cents per M cu. ft.
of gas sold.

PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION - FIRST DISTRICT
The Astoria Light, Heat and Power Company
Officers.- President, Wm. H. Bradley; Vice-President, Horace W. Fuller; Assist-
ant Secretary, C. M. Carbonell; Treasurer, C. G. M. Thomas; Auditor, F. L. Lam-
brecht; Controller, R. A. Carter.
Directors.- George F. Baker, N. F. Brady, George B. Cortelyou, Donald G.
Geddes, Wm. Rockefeller, John A. Garver.
Main Business Office.-130 E. 15th Street, New York City.
Report verified by W. H. Bradley, President, March 15, 1921.
THE BRONX GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY
Incorporated August 16, 1893. Supplies water gas and electricity in that section
of the Borough of the Bronx lying east of the Bronx river, formerly the old town
of Westchester, exclusive of the incorporated village of Williamsbridge.
Respondent reports that the stock books were not closed during the year 1920,
but that on February 21, 1921, a list of stockholders was compiled. As of this
date the Consolidated Gas Co. was entitled to 4,645 votes, out of a total of 4,865.
Important Changes during the Year.- Securities purchased: First and refunding
mortgage bonds, at par, $20,000; for sinking fund, $13,000, and casualty and insurance
fund, $7,000.
Power contract with United Electric Light and Power Co., effective November 10,
1920; termination to be on two years' written notice after the first year; considera-
tion, the investment in equipment and apparatus; amount of business done during
1920, $12,726.49.
No new powers or franchises were acquired.

1 This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation. i. e., amortization of the cost
of perishable property (see Depreciation, page 779) but it reports on the liabili
ties side of the balance sheet the reserve "Accrued Amortization of Capital" that
contains accumulations made in former years through the prescribed account.
As no segregation is made of such accumulations, the entire credit balances are
necessarily deducted from fixed capital or cost of property in the same manner
as accumulations under the prescribed account. Accrued amortization of capital.
representing the expired life or amortized portion of fixed capital outlay.

Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of July 23, 1912, this
company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides for a
charge to" General amortization - Gas" of 12 cents per M. cu. ft. of gas sold, and
for a charge to " General amortization - electric" of 1 cent per kw. hr. of electric
current sold [less cost of actual repairs]. Supplementary to the provisions of
this rule the company added pro rata at the close of the year a sufficient amount to
make the total credit to reserve $30,000 for the year. This procedure was followed
down to and including the year 1917. In 1918 and 1919, nothing was reserved for
general amortization. In 1920, 4 cents per kw. hr. sold was reserved for "General
amortization - Electric " and 6 cents per M. cu. ft. sold was reserved for General
amortization -gas."

BROOKLYN BOROUGH GAS COMPANY
Incorporated February 21, 1908. Supplies water gas in the 31st ward, Borough of
Brooklyn. The company is controlled by Caroline Foerderer Artman, administra-
trix of the estate of Robert H. Foerderer, the control having been acquired June
1, 1907.
Important Changes during the Year.- Rate per M charged to consumers increased
on April 1, from $1.10 to $1.15; on August 1, from $1.15 to $1.40. No additional
powers or franchises were acquired.

THE BROOKLYN EDISON COMPANY, INCORPORATED
The Kings County Electric Light & Power Co. changed its corporate name on
January 10, 1919, to the Brooklyn Edison Co. Inc. and on January 27, 1919, merged
the Edison Electric Illuminating Co. of Brooklyn.
番
​Total number of votes cast at the last general meeting, February 24, 1920, was
120,962. The ten largest stockholders and their share holdings as of the close of the
year, December 31, 1920, were: Estate of Anthony N. Brady, 27,578; Brooklyn Edison
Investment Fund. 5,826; Estate of Hugh C. Grant, 4,453; J. & S. Ferguson, 3,540;
Brooklyn Trust Co., 2,705; Genevieve C. Brady, 2,469; Eugene C. Woods, 2,462;
Win. H. Buckley, 2,459; Thos. E. Murray, 2,043; John Day Jackson, 1,830. Each
share represents one vote and has a par value of $100.

This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost of
perishable property (See depreciation, page 794); but it reports on the liabilities
side of the balance sheet certain reserves" Renewals and Replacements" and
"Contingency Reserve," that contain accumulations made in former years through
the prescribed accounts. As no segregation is made of such accumulations, the
entire credit balances are necessarily deducted from fixed capital or cost of property
in the same manner as accumulations under the prescribed account. Accrued
amortization of capital, representing the expired life or amortized portion of fixed
capital outlay.
Bonds of the Edison Electric Illuminating Co. of Brooklyn and the Kings County
Electric Light & Power Co. having a par value of $742,000 and a book value of
$796.960 (see Miscellaneous Investments, page 788) are held by the Central Union
Trust Co. of New York as trustee, for the benefit of the Edison Selling Stockholders,
having been acquired by it with the $1,000,000 fund deposited with it by the Kings
County Elec. L. & P. Co. as a guarantee of the payment to the Edison Selling Stock-
holders of the interest on its purchase money bonds issued to them in exchange for
their stock in the Edison Electric Illuminating Company of Brooklyn.

[The Amsterdam Electric Light, Heat & Power Co. acquired subways from the
Municipal Electric Light Co. merged Oct. 30, 1899, by the Edison Electric Illuminot-
ing Co. of Brooklyn. As purchase price of the subways it gave a 6% note of $20.000,
June 29, 1899, it leased the subways to the Municipal Electric Light Co. for a
nominal rental of $1,200 a year.] The interest accrual of $1,200 is offset by the
rental of $1,200. In 1919 only $1,100 was accrued but in 1920 an adjustment was made
by accruing $1,300.
Includes $5.750 bonds held on behalf of employees who subscribed on the install-
ment plan for which the company gave its notes to a bank.

46
Depreciation. For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting
for depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. The company's procedure,
however, is not in accordance with the requirements of the accounting order. It
charges an account Renewals and replacements," with six-tenths of a cent per
kilowatt hour per month for all current sold to general consumers. Capital retire-
ments are charged to the reserve "Renewals and replacements" instead of to the
prescribed account "Accrued amortization of capital."

## p. (#795) ################################################

GAS COMPANIES AND ELECTRICAL COMPANIES
795
THE BROOKLYN UNION GAS COMPANY
(INCLUDING EQUITY GAS COMPANY)
Incorporated September 7, 1895. [On November 4, 1895, the respondent acquired
all the property, franchises and other rights of the following companies: Brook-
lyn Gas Light Co., Citizens Gas Co.,¹ The Fulton Municipal Gas Co., Metropolitan
Gas Light Co., Nassau Gas Light Co., Peoples Gas Light Co., and Williamsburgh Gas
Light Co.]
The company supplies water gas in the Borough of Brooklyn, 1st to 28th wards,
inclusive, Windsor Terrace section of the 29th ward and entire 32nd ward. Service
was begun November 4, 1895.
This company has sole control of the following companies through ownership
of the entire capital stock, viz.:
Equity Gas Co.
The Flatbush Gas Co.
The Jamaica Gas Light Co.
The Newtown Gas Co.
The Richmond Hill and Queens County Gas Co.
The Woodhaven Gas Light Co.
The respondent holds possession, as tenant at will, of the gas works of the Equity
Gas Co. situated at Maspeth and Porter avenues. [The other companies act as
distributing branch agencies for respondent, which manufactures all gas supplied
by them to the public.]
The ten principal stockholders, December 31, 1920, were: Whitehouse and Co.,
6,854 shares; D. G. Legget, 6,490; Fahnestock & Co., 4,000; Brooklyn Trust Co.,
2,327; Thomas Reed, 2,300; Estate of A. Scheftel by E. K. Scheftel, Exec., Home
Insurance Co., 2,000; James N. Jarvie, 1,885; Cara L. Broughton, 1,880; Anne E.
Benjamin, 1,600.
Important Changes During Year.- Consolidated mortgage bonds of the Union
Gas Light Co., amounting to $38,000, matured on January 1, 1920, and were retired;
a like amount of consolidated mortgage bonds of The Brooklyn Union Gas Co.
were received in exchange from the trustee.
For additions to capital account, see Details of Construction Projects Completed
and Fixed Capital Installed During the Year.
Rate for gas changed from 80 cents per M cu. ft. to 97 cents per M cu. ft.,
effective March 1, 1920, and from 97 cents per M cu. ft. to $1.10 per M cu. ft.,
effective December 1, 1920, as authorized by orders of the United States District
Court, dated February 25, 1920, and November 26, 1920, respectively.
Effective January 1, 1920, the rate charged for gas to subsidiary companies was
changed from the flat rate of 65 cents per M cu. ft. to (1) cost of manufacture to
this company plus transmission pumping plus 16 cents per M. cu. ft. to The New-
town Gas Co. and The Flatbush Gas Co., and (2) cost of manufacture to this
company plus transmission pumping plus 21 cents per M cu. ft. to The Richmond
Hill & Queens County Gas Light Co., The Jamaica Gas Light Co. and The Wood-
haven Gas Light Co.
Thomas P. Payne elected Auditor, October 14, 1920, in place of J. Gilmer, deceased.
No new powers or franchises were acquired.

Contingent Liabilities.-Amount of bond filed in accordance with the provisions
of the order of the United States District Court, dated November 26, 1920, allowing
the withdrawal of the moneys on deposit with Richard Welling, Esq., Special Master,
$1,500,000.
Amount of Joint and Several bonds in which The Brooklyn Union Gas Company
joins with The Newtown Gas Company, Richmond Hill & Queens County Gas
Light Company, The Jamaica Gas Light Company, and The Woodhaven Gas Light
This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost
of perishable property (See Depreciation, page 803); but it reports on the liabilities
side of the balance sheet certain reserves (Accrued Amortization of Capital" and
"Contingencies "), that contain accumulations made in former years through the
prescribed account. As no segregation is made of such accumulations, the entire
credit balances are necessarily deducted from fixed capital or cost of property in
the same manner as accumulations under the prescribed account, Accrued amortiza-
tion of capital, representing the expired life or amortized portion of fixed capital
outlay.
Includes among other items, $1,253,492.23 for gas sent out December 10-31, 1920, not
billed; $28.303.64 for gas on hand at close of year, and $206,222.06 for amounts un-
collected during the year from consumers, in excess of the statutory rate.
The difference between statutory rate of eighty cents, and the amounts billed
consumers for commercial metered lighting and prepaid gas, impounded in accordance
with United States District Court Orders of February 25, 1920 and November 26, 1920,
$1,956,854.93; interest on monies impounded. $14,177.38; total, $1,971,032.31.

Depreciation. For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of April 15, 1909, this
company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides for a
charge to "General amortization gas and concurrent credit to "Accrued
""
amortization of capital" of 15 cents per M cu. ft. sold (less the cost of actual re-
pairs.) The company's precedure, however, is not in accordance either with this
rule or with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead, the bulk of the
amount accumulated in the prescribed reserve is now held in an unauthorized" Con-
tingencies reserve and the company charges "General amortization" and concur-
rently credits " Accrued amortization of capital" with 1.5¢ per M cu. ft of gas sold
for the amortization of capital invested in plant withdrawn from service."

ADDENDUM
EQUITY GAS COMPANY
The Brooklyn Union Gas Company by virtue of the ownership of securities, holds
the physical property of the Equity Gas Co. Without formal lease. For a state-
ment of its corporate history, securities issued and property owned see 1917 report.
The expenditures made on its plant by the Brooklyn Union Gas Co. and charged to
the Brooklyn Union's fixed capital accounts have been reported as follows:

THE BRUSH ELECTRIC ILLUMINATING COMPANY OF NEW YORK
LESSOR
Incorporated in February, 1881. Obtained franchise from City of New York
[May 3, 1881].
[The company is not operating. In an agreement dated June 25, 1900, it turned
over its plant to the New York Edison Co., which has since then been sold as
junk. The company has no apparatus or machinery of any kind.]¹
The New York Edison Co. supplies its consumers with electric current under
an operating agreement dated May 2, 1904 [superseding the agreement dated June 25,
1900], amended November 23, 1905, and renewed for 10 years from May 1, 1914.
Sole control of this company is held by the United Electric Light & Power Co.,
through ownership of its entire capital stock acquired during 1890-1905 [which is
pledged to Union Trust Co., trustee under the mortgage of United Electric Light &
Power Co., dated June 15, 1894].¹
Important Changes during the Year.- A lease of the building at 204/10 Elizabeth
street was entered into on May 1, 1920, with the United Electric Light & Power
Company at an annual rental of $12,500, expiring May 1, 1941. The United Electric
Light & Power Company is to assume all responsibility for repairs, taxes, water-
rents, etc. No additional powers or franchises were acquired.

## p. (#807) ################################################

GAS COMPANIES AND ELECTRICAL COMPANIES
807
CENTRAL UNION GAS COMPANY
Incorporated July 13, 1897, and on that date began to supply gas in the 23d ward
of The Bronx.
The entire capital stock of the company with the exception of 35 shares [held
for the purpose of qualifying seven directors], was acquired March 11, 1898, by
the New Amsterdam Gas Co. [a subsidiary company of the Consolidated Gas Co.
of New York.]
Respondent owns 7,600 shares of the capital stock of the Northern Union Gas Co.
[The remaining 7,400 shares are held by the New Amsterdam Gas Co.]
Important Changes during the Year.- Rate changes were instituted as follows:
Under preliminary injunction granted by the federal court, a rate of $1 per
M cu. ft. was put in effect July 1, 1920. Under a modifying order of the court,
this rate was increased to $1.10 per M cu. ft. December 1, 1920. These court orders
contain provisions for the impounding, pending final judgment in the action, of
the amount collected in excess of the statutory rate of 80 cents per M cu. ft. either
by depositing the monies in the court or by the deposit of substituted security.
No additional powers or franchises were acquired.

1 This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e. amortization of cost of
perishable property (see paragraph on Depreciation, page 813) but it reports on the
liabilities side of the balance sheet certain reserves (Renewals and replacements
plant withdrawn from service; Contingency reserve), that contain accumulations
made in former years through the prescribed accounts. As no segregation is made
of such accumulations the entire credit balances are necesarily deducted from fixed
capital or cost of property in the same manner as accumulations. Under the pre-
scribed account Accrued amortization of capital, representing the expired life or
amortized portion of fixed capital outlay.

-
Depreciation. For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of March 1, 1909,
this company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides
for a charge to "General amortization Gas" and concurrent credit to "Accrued
amortization of capital" of 18.17 cents per M cu. ft. sold [less the cost of actual
repairs]. The company's procedure, however, is not in accordance either with
this rule or with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead it has included
in operating expenses without authority from the Commission the item, Renewals
and replacements - plant withdrawn from service," charging thereto in 1920 3 cents
per M cu. ft. of gas sold.

## p. (#814) ################################################

814
-
PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION FIRST DISTRICT
Central Union Gas Company
Materials and Residuals. See Tables IX and XI.
Accidents.- See Tables XXVII and XXVIII.
Employees and Wages.- See Tables XXX and XXXI.
Officers.- President, William H. Bradley; Vice-President and Secretary, Louis H.
Werner; Assistant Secretary, Charles G. Duffy; Treasurer, Philip J. Schneider;
Assistant Treasurer and Superintendent of Distribution, Arthur H. Hall.
Directors.- Nicholas F. Brady, George B. Cortelyou, William Rockefeller, R. A.
Carter, George W. Doane, William H. Bradley, J. A. Garver.
Main Business Office.-529 Courtlandt Avenue, The Bronx, New York City.
Report verified by Philip J. Schneider, Treasurer, March 15, 1921.
CONSOLIDATED GAS COMPANY OF NEW YORK
Organized November 10, 1884, under chapter 367 of the Laws of 1884, through
the consolidation of the following companies: Manhattan Gas Light Company,
New York Gas Light Company, Metropolitan Gas Light Company, Knickerbocker
Gas Light Company, Harlem Gas Light Company and Municipal Gas Light Com-
pany. Since the date of its organization the company has supplied gas in the
Borough of Manhattan and claims the right to operate in the Borough of The
Bronx. It supplies mixed coal and water gas. (For controlled companies, see
schedule of Investments, and previous Reports.)
Total number of votes cast at the last general meeting held on January 26, 1920
was 588,801. The ten largest stockholders and their share-holdings, as of December
31, 1908 (last reported), were: Mutual Life Insurance Co., 15,000; Wm. C. Rockwell,
13,400; Anthony N. Brady, 9,666; Henry J. Cammann, 9,500; J. M. Amory & Son,
7,800; Pearl & Co., 7,100; Edey, Brown & Sanderson, 6,700; David G. Leggett, 6,267;
Hugh J. Grant, 6,065; Home Insurance Co., 6,000.
Important Changes during the Year.- No extensions of system or plant have been
made, excepting such as are reflected under Fixed Capital Installed since December
31, 1908. Securities were acquired from the following companies for the cancellation
of a like amount of their floating debt to respondent: Capital stock, from the New
York Edison Co., 35,000 shares at par; ten year 7% debentures, from the United
Electric Light & Power Co., $8,825,000; from the Westchester Lighting Co., $3,390,000;
from the New York & Queens Electric Light & Power Co., $3,400,000.
The company purchased 60 shares of preferred and 36 shares of common stock
of the Standard Gas Light Co., 45 shares of preferred and 61% shares of common
stock of New Amsterdam Gas Co., and 20 shares of stock of the New York Edison
Co., for $70,000; 246 shares of stock of the Ball Electric Illumination Co. for $17,500;
the entire cost of which, $87,500, was charged to surplus. Company acquired at par,
$480,000 of collateral trust notes of Westchester Lighting Co. and received from
the United Electric Light & Power Co., $1,013,998.32 in cancellation of floating debt
(notes $962,479.67, loans $50,000 and interest, $1,518.65). The company on February 1,
1920, issued five-year secured 7% convertible gold bonds, par value $25,000,000, to
retire 6% convertible debentures on that date and on December 1, 1920, issued
$25.000,000 par value 8% one year secured gold notes.
Under a preliminary injunction granted by the federal court, the rate for gas
was changed from 80 cents per M. cu. ft. to $1.00 per M. cu. ft. effective July 1, 1920.
Pursuant to final decrees entered by the Court on August 4 and 11, 1920, a rate of
$1.20 was put into effect as of August 6, 1920. Under the terms of this decree
the sums collected in excess of 80¢ per M. cubic feet or substituted security
therefor, are required to be held by the Court pending the outcome of the appeal
and the fixation of a new rate by this Commission.
On January 29, 1920, Lewis B. Gawtry resigned as vice-president and the following
appointments were made: C. G. M. Thomas as vice president, H. M. Brundage as
secretary. F. H. Nickerson as assistant secretary, and B. Whiteley as treasurer.
On October 28, 1920, J. H. N. Armstrong was appointed assistant treasurer and
H. C. Davidson comptroller. No new powers or franchises were acquired.

1 This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e. amortization of the cost of
perishable property (see Depreciation, page 824); but reports on the liabilities side
of the balance sheet certain reserves ("Renewals and replacements - plant with-
drawn from service" and "Contingency ") that contain accumulations made in
former years through the prescribed accounts. As no segregation is made of such
accumulations, the entire credit balances are necessarily deducted from fixed capi-
tal or cost of property in the same manner as accumulations under the prescribed
account, Accrued amortization of capital, representing the expired life or amortized
portion of fixed capital outlay.

Depreciation. For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of March 5, 1909,
this company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides for
a charge to General amortization
gas and concurrent credit to "Accrued
amortization of capital" of 16.39¢ per M cu. ft. of gas sold [less the cost of actual
repairs]. The company's procedure, however, is not in accordance either
with this rule or with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead it has
included in operating expenses without authority from the Commission the item.

1920, 32 cents per M cu. ft. of gas sold.

## p. (#828) ################################################

828
FIRST DISTRICT
PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION
-
CONSOLIDATED TELEGRAPH AND ELECTRICAL SUBWAY COMPANY
incorporated December 26, 1885. The company was organized for the purpose of
constructing, operating and maintaining subways for the reception of electrical
conductors of duly authorized electrical companies doing business in the City of
New York. The respondent owns all the subways located in the boroughs of
Manhattan and The Bronx used for high tension electric light and power purposes.
The New York Gas & Electric Light, Heat & Power Co. in 1898 acquired a
majority of the capital stock of this company at the same time that it acquired
a majority of the capital stock of the Edison Electric Illuminating Co. of New York,
and when the New York Edison Co. was formed in 1901 by consolidation of the
New York Gas & Electric Light, Heat & Power Co. and the Edison Electric
Illuminating Co. of New York, it inherited control of the Consolidated Telegraph &
Electrical Subway Co., which control it retains.¹
Important Changes during the Year.- Debenture bonds were issued in the amount
of $760,000 at 7% bearing date of January 2, 1920, for payment of 1919 construction
work, payable on 30 days' written notice.
On July 1, 1920, E. S. Callahan succeeded E. R. Ginnly as Chief Engineer. No
new rights or franchises were acquired.

1 From documents on file with this Commission.
This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost
of perishable property (see Depreciation, page 831); but it reports on the liabilities
side of the balance sheet a "Contingency reserve which is similar in character
(although more comprehensive) to the prescribed account. This reserve credit
balance is accordingly deducted from fixed capital (cost of property) account,
leaving a remainder which may or may not exceed the net fixed capital - which
in the normal case represents the unimpaired investment or unexpired capital
outlay.
3 Brick, pipe, tile, etc.

income accrued during year, $1,062.50.

## p. (#833) ################################################

GAS COMPANIES AND ELECTRICAL COMPANIES
833
Consolidated Telegraph and Electrical Subway Company
Type of Ducts.- Fibre, iron (different kinds) Johnstone system, vitrified tile, wood
and concrete. Distributing lines of one duct are laid bare and in concrete; others
are concreted. The trunks with few exceptions are laid in concrete.
Accidents. See Table XXVII and XXIX.
Employees and Wages.- See Table XXXI.
Officers.- President, Henry J. Hemmens; Vice-President, Thomas E. Murray;
Secretary, William F. Reardon; Treasurer, A. W. Middleton; Assistant Treasurer,
J. A. Shea; Attorney, Beardsley, Hemmens & Taylor; General Manager, A. W.
Middleton; Chief Engineer, E. S. Callahan.
Directors. George B. Cortelyou, Nicholas F. Brady, Henry J. Hemmens, Lewis B.
Gawtry, Thomas E. Murray, Samuel A. Beardsley, Arthur W. Middleton.
Main Business Office.- 54 Lafayette street, New York City.
Report verified by Henry J. Hemmens, President, March 18, 1921.
EMPIRE CITY SUBWAY COMPANY (LIMITED)
Incorporated July 12, 1890. Its business is solely the construction, maintenance
and leasing of subways for low tension electrical conductors in the streets and
avenues of the boroughs of Manhattan and the Bronx. [Under contract dated May
15, 1891, the city of New York received an option to purchase all the subways, con-
duits and ducts of the company. Another provision was that when the cumulative
annual profits of the company should exceed 10% upon the actual cash capital in-
vested in the plant, the excess of such profit should be paid into the treasury of
the City of New York.] The control of the company, through ownership of its
capital stock, was acquired by the New York Telephone Co., on or about January
21, 1897.
Important Changes During the Year.- Capital stock to the amount of $155,000 and
$155,000 first mortgage telephone and telegraph bonds were issued and sold at par
for cash to pay in part for subways constructed during the year 1919.
powers or franchises were acquired.

THE FLATBUSH GAS COMPANY
Incorporated April 2, 1864. The franchises and property of the Knickerbocker
Electric Light & Power Co. were acquired by purchase December 27, 1893.1
Of these quantities, 1,403,427 trench feet and 20,221,421 duct feet are in the
Borough of Manhattan and 430,562 trench feet and 5,540,377 duct feet are in the
Borough of The Bronx.
Of this number, 12,683,040 duct feet are unoccupied.
1 For corporate history see annual report of the Commission, 1910, Vol. I, pp. 288-291.


## p. (#838) ################################################

838
FIRST DISTRICT
PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION
The Flatbush Gas Company
The company supplies electricity and water gas in Ward 29, Borough of Brooklyn,
and electri ty on Ocean parkway from Foster avenue to the Atlantic ocean.
Respondent states that it has no record as to when gas was first supplied, but
that electricity was first supplied in 1893. It has no gas plant, and purchases gas
from the Brooklyn Union Gas Co.
Stock control of the company is held by the Brooklyn Union Gas Co., which
acquired the respondent's entire capital stock on May 31, 1897.
Important Changes during the Year.- Conduits laid during year, 23,202 trench feet;
132,521 duct feet, 188 manholes; distribution mains laid during the year, 5,584 feet.
The rate paid to the Brooklyn Union Gas Co. for gas purchased was changed
from 65¢ per M cu. ft. to a price based on the manufacturing cost of the Brooklyn
Union Gas Co., plus transmission pumping, plus 16 cents per M cu ft., effective
January 1, 1920.
The rate charged to private consumers was changed from $0.80 to $1.10 per M
cu. ft., effective December 1, 1920, as authorized by order of U. S. District Court
dated November 26, 1920, the increase being impounded pending decision of the
U. S. Supreme Court.
J. W. Cornwell, Jr., resigned as Second Assistant Treasurer, effective December
31, 1919. J. Gilmer, Auditor, deceased July 9, 1920. J. T. White elected Assistant
Secretary, effective February 26, 1920. M. O. Davis elected Second Assistant Treas-
urer, effective February 26, 1920. J. I. Blanchfield elected Assistant Auditor, effective
February 26, 1920. T. P. Payne elected Auditor, effective October 14, 1920.
No additional powers or franchises were acquired.

Hours burning per year, 3,962, New York city schedule. Gas per lamp hour, 3½
cu. ft.
Operating Expenses.- For details, see Tables XXXIII and XXXVI.
-
Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of April 15, 1909, this
company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides for a
charge to "General amortization — electric" of 3 cents per kw.hr. of current sold
[less the cost of actual repairs] and a charge to "General amortization
Gas "
of 13 cents per M cu. ft. sold [less the cost of actual repairs] and concurrent
credits to " Accrued amortization of capital." The company's procedure, however,
is not in accordance either with this rule or with the requirements of the account-
ing order. Instead the bulk of the amount accumulated in the prescribed reserves
is now held in two unauthorized "Contingencies reserves, and the company charges
"General amortization" and concurrently credits "Accrued amortization of capi-
tal" with 5¢ per kw.hr. of electricity sold and 1.5¢ per M cu. ft. of gas sold "for the
amortization of capital invested in plant withdrawn from service."
Taxes. See Table XLIII.

THE JAMAICA GAS LIGHT COMPANY
Incorporated February 18, 1856.
It furnishes water gas to Ward 4, in the section known as Jamaica, Borough of
Queens, New York City. The entire supply is purchased from the Brooklyn Union
Gas Co., as the company has no manufacturing plant.
Stock control of the respondent is held by the Brooklyn Union Gas Co., which
acquired its capital stock in July, 1897.
Important Changes during the Year. The rate paid to the Brooklyn Union Gas
Co. for gas purchased was changed from 65 cents per M. to a price based on the
manufacturing cost of The Brooklyn Union Gas Co., plus transmission pumping,
plus 21 cents per M., effective January 1, 1920.
T. P. Payne elected auditor, effective October 14, 1920. No new powers or franchises
were acquired.

1 This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost
of perishable property (see Depreciation, page 849) but reports on the liabilities
side of the balance sheet certain reserves ("Accrued amortization of capital," and

credit balances are necessarily deducted from fixed capital or cost of property

## p. (#849) ################################################

GAS COMPANIES AND ELECTRICAL COMPANIES
849
The Jamaica Gas Light Company
Depreciation. For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of April 15, 1909, this
company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides for a
charge to "General amortization - Gas" and concurrent credit to "Accrued
amortization of capital" of 13 cents per M cu. ft. sold (less the cost of actual re-
pairs). The company's procedure, however, is not in accordance either with this
rule or with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead, the bulk of the
amount accumulated in the prescribed reserve is now held in an unauthorized
"Contingency " reserve and the company charges "General amortization" and
concurrently credits "Accrued amortization of capital with 1.5¢ per M cu. ft. of
gas sold "for the amortization of capital invested in plant withdrawn from service."
Taxes.- See Table XLIII.
Rent Revenues.- Rent of land, Beaver street, Jamaica, Brooklyn Union Gas Co.,
lessee, $1,755.89; taxes applicable to same, $1,405.89; net rent revenue, $350; office
14-20 Union ave., Jamaica, lessees, John Hancock Life Ins. Co., $1,500; the Jamaica
Water Supply Co., $783.33; Richmond Hill & Queens County Gas Light Co., $1,250;
The Woodhaven Gas Light Co., $2,000; less expenses, $3,208.93; net revenue, $2,674.40.
MISCELLANEOUS INFORMATION
Works and Holders.- Respondent has no manufacturing plant or holders.

KINGS COUNTY LIGHTING COMPANY
Incorporated May 26, 1904. On July 1, 1904, the company absorbed by merger
the Kings County Gas & Illuminating Co., which was incorporated December 18, 1889.
Respondent supplies water gas to the 30th ward and a small part of the 31st ward
of Brooklyn. Gas for municipal and general consumption was first supplied in
1891.
Total number of votes cast at the last general meeting, January 27, 1920, was
17,387. The ten largest stockholders and their share-holdings as of the close of
the year, December 31, 1920, were: F. M. Tait, 6,989; Ralph Elsman, 6,333; N. F.
Brady, 1,818; C. A. Billings, J. C. Brady and F. M. Tait, trustees, 1,520; Julia M. M.
Grant, trustee, 984; James Shevlin, 492; Laura J. Roche, 295; Helen A. Courtney,
295; Edith von Girtig, 278; D. Olcott, 200.
Important Changes during the Year. The following general officers were elected:
President, Ralph Elsman; Secretary, A. J. Gonnoud; Treasurer and Second Vice-
President, Paul Bucher; First Vice-President, Charles H. Dickey. Voting trust
(C. A. Billings, J. C. Brady and T. M. Tait, Trustees) was dissolved November,
1920; 1,520 shares still remain on record in Trustees' name.
Rate for gas was changed to $1.50 per M cu. ft., effective October 23, 1920.
No additional powers or franchises were acquired,

Contingent Liabilities.- The above surplus, $296,717.46, is subject to a contingent
liability of $568,320.35 being the difference between 95¢ and 80¢ per M cu. ft.,
This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost
of perishable property (See Depreciation, page 855); but it reports on the lia-
bilities side of the balance sheet certain reserves (Plant withdrawn from service;
Contingency reserve) that contain accumulations made in former years through
the prescribed accounts. As no segregation is made of such accumulations, the
entire credit balances are necessarily deducted from fixed capital or cost of
property in the same manner as accumulations under the prescribed account,
Accrued amortization of capital, representing the expired life or amortized portion
of fixed capital outlay.
[By order of the Supreme Court dated August 7, 1918, the company was author-
ized to use for specified purposes the special deposit representing the difference
between the statutory rate of 80¢ per M cu. ft., effective July 1, 1916, and the
95¢ rate collected, on condition that it deposit its 5% first and refunding mortgage
bonds of equal amount, and that it continue to deposit additional bonds to cover
further excess charges. These bond deposits amounted to $569,000 par value as of
December 31, 1920.]

11 The 80¢ statutory rate for gas has not been enforced as to this company due
to an injunction obtained from the State Supreme Court under which the com-
pany collected 95¢ and prior to March 29. 1920, deposited with the Empire Trust
Company bonds equal to the excess over 80 cents. These deposits were increased
by $16,843.68 in 1920. On Oct. 19, 1920, the Federal District Court declared the 80-
cent rate confiscatory, whereupon (Oct. 23, 1920) the company increased the rate
collected to $1.50 per M cu. ft. An appeal from this decision has been taken to
the U. S. Supreme Court. The company's income and surplus accounts fail to
reflect the contingent asset character of the amount collected in excess of the
statutory rate.

Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of July 31, 1916,
this company filled with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides for a
charge to " General amortization - gas," and concurrent credit to "Accrued amor-
tization of capital of 11.5 cents per M cu. ft. sold (less the cost of actual
repairs). The company's procedure, however, is not in accordance either with
this rule or with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead it has included
in operating expenses without authority from the Commission the item " Provision
for plant withdrawn from service," charging thereto 1e per M cu. ft. of gas sold
January 1, 1920, to June 30, 1920, and 3¢ per M cu. ft. of gas sold July 1, 1920, to
December 31, 1920. The company has made an appropriation from surplus to
Contingency reserve of 2¢ per M cu. ft. sold.

MISCELLANEOUS INFORMATION
Power Plant.- This company is operating under a contract with the Forty-first

of the latter is used by this company for the generation of power sold to customers.

## p. (#859) ################################################

GAS COMPANIES AND ELECTRICAL COMPANIES
859
Long Acre Electric Light and Power Company
Franchise Requirements.— The company shall furnish free to the city one full arc
light for every fifty furnished to other consumers. Also for any permit to open the
streets the company shall pay one cent per lineal foot of streets occupied under
such permit.
Accidents.- None.
Employees and Wages.- Officers, part time only. Number of all other employees
at close of year, 8; total compensation paid to employees during the year, $12,685.10.
Officers.- President, P. G. Gossler; Vice-President, M. J. Murphy; Secretary and
Treasurer, F. B. Lasher; General Manager or Superintendent, C. Gast.
Directors,- W. E. Fulcher, P. G. Gossler, F. B. Lasher, A. B. Leach, M. J. Murphy,
C. T. Smith.
Main Business Office.- 62 Cedar street, New York City.
Report verified by F. B. Lasher, Treasurer, March 11, 1921.
NEW AMSTERDAM GAS COMPANY.
The report of this company combines with it the data of the East River Gas Co.
of Long Island City. (See Addendum.)
The New Amsterdam Gas Co., Equitable Gas Light Co. of New York and New
York & East River Gas Co. were consolidated on March 4, 1898, to form the present
New Amsterdam Gas Co. Gas service by the consolidated company was first sup-
plied on that date.
The company controls through title to all or to a majority of the capital stock
the Central Union Gas Co., East River Gas Co. of Long Island City, New York
Carbide & Acetylene Co. (inchoate) and, indirectly, Northern Union Gas Co. (See
Miscellaneous Investments.)
Stock control of the New Amsterdam Gas Co. was acquired July 23, 1900, by the
Consolidated Gas Co. which on December 31, 1920, was the holder of record of
121,092 shares of respondents common stock and 89,625 shares of preferred stock.¹
Important Changes during the Year- Renewed lease with Consolidated Gas Co.
for space on 1st, 2nd and 3rd floors in building at 130 E. 15th street from March 1,
1920 to December 31, 1920 at an annual rental of $19,200. Leased from New York &
Queens Electric Light & Power Co., for 10 years, store No. 3, Queens plaza, Long
Island City at an annual rental of $2,500 from May 1, 1920, to April 30, 1925; of
$2,812.50 from May 1, 1925 to April 30, 1927; of $3,125 from May 1, 1927 to April 30,
1930.
Under a preliminary injunction granted by the Federal court, a rate of $1.00 per
M cu. ft. was put in effect July 1, 1920. Under a modifying order of the court, this
rate was increased to $1.10 per M cu. ft. as of December 1, 1920.
No additional powers or franchises were acquired.

[The Consolidated Gas Co. reports in addition ownership of 511 shares of com-

2 This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of cost of
perishable property (see Depreciation, page 866), but it reports on the liabilities
side of the balance sheet certain reserves (Renewals and replacements - plant
withdrawn from service Contingency reserve) that contain accumulations
made in former years through the prescribed account. As no segregation is made
of such accumulations, the entire credit balances are necessarily deducted from
fixed capital or cost of property in the same manner as accumulations under the
prescribed account, Accrued amortization of capital, representing the expired life
or amortized portion of fixed capital outlay.

Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of March 1, 1909,
this company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides
for a charge to "General amortization
gas
and concurrent credit to "Accrued
amortization of capital" of 18.75 cents per M. cu. ft. sold [less the cost of actual
repairs]. The company's procedure, however, is in accordance neither with this
rule nor with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead it has included
in operating expenses without authority from the Commission the item, Renewals
and replacements - plant withdrawn from service," charging thereto in 1920, 3 cents
per M cu. ft. of gas sold.

W. C. Morris, Edgar Palmer, N. F. Brady, George B. Cortelyou, J. A. Garver.
Main Business Office.- 20 Webster Avenue, Long Island City.
Report verified by M. M. Graham, Secretary and Treasurer, March 21, 1921
THE NEWTOWN GAS COMPANY
Incorporated April 6, 1891. The company supplies water gas to ward 2, Borough
of Queens, City of New York, (the former town of Newtown). It has no manu-
facturing plant and purchases gas from the Brooklyn Union Gas Co.
Its entire capital stock is owned by The Brooklyn Union Gas Co.
Important Changes during the Year. The construction of a new 10,000,000 cu. ft.
gas holder was begun and will be completed during the year 1921. The rate paid
to the Brooklyn Union Gas Co. for gas purchased was changed from 65¢ per M cu. ft
to a price based on the manufacturing cost of the Brooklyn Union Gas Co. plus
transmission pumping, plus 16¢ per M cu. ft., effective January 1, 1920. The rate
charged to private consumers was changed from $1.00 to $1.10 per M. cu. ft., effective
December 1, 1920, as authorized by U. S. District Court dated November 26, 1920.
T. P. Payne elected Auditor, effective October 14, 1920. No additional powers or
franchises were acquired.

*
Operating Expenses. For details, see Table XXXIII.
-
Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducng these abstracts. Under date of April 15, 1909, this
company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides for a
charge to "General amortization
- gas "and concurrent credit to "Accrued amorti-
zation of capital" of 13 cents per M cu. ft. sold (less the cost of actual repairs.)
The company's precedure, however, is not in accordance either with this rule or
with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead, the bulk of the amount
accumulated in the prescribed reserve is now held in an unauthorized "Contin-
gencies" reserve and the company charges "General amortization" and concur-
rently credits Accrued amortization of capital with 1.5 per M cu. ft. of gas sold
for the amortization of capital invested in plant withdrawn from service.

PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION-FIRST DISTRICT
NEW YORK AND QUEENS ELECTRIC LIGHT AND POWER COMPANY
Incorporated July 23, 1900. Stock control of the respondent is held by the Con-
solidated Gas Company of New York which acquired in 1913, 7,612 shares of the
12,500 shares of preferred stock and 9,659 shares of the 12,500 shares of common stock
outstanding.
Important Changes during the Year.- Ten year Debenture Gold bonds amounting
to $3,400,000 were issued December 31, 1920 for Construction expenditures,
and were accepted by the Consolidated Gas Co. of New York at par for cash
(Issued by authority of Public Service Commission, 1st District, December 21, 1920.
Case No. 2461). Mortgage, amounting to $75,000, was assumed by respondent in pur-
chase of property, at East River and Vernon Avenue, Long Island City, known as
Lot 49, Block 477, Vol. 8, Ward 1. Mortgages amounting to $20,000 assumed by re-
spondent in purchase of property known as 70-72 Radde Street, Long Island City.
No new powers or franchises were acquired.

Report verified by Ray Palmer, President, March 14, 1921.
NEW YORK AND QUEENS GAS COMPANY
Incorporated July 12, 1904. Respondent supplies water-gas to the Third ward,
Borough of Queens, including the former villages of Flushing, College Point, White-
stone and Bayside. It also has the right to operate in the former towns of New-
town and Jamaica. Stock control of the company was acquired in 1913 by the Con-
solidated Gas Co. of New York which is reported by the respondent to be the holder
of record of 5,400 shares out of a total of 6,000 shares outstanding.¹
Important changes during the Year.- Distribution system extended to Little Neck.
For gas supplied on and after November 19, 1920, the price of $1.25 per M cu. ft.,
plus a monthly service charge of 75 cents was charged. Pursuant to the judgment
entered by the United States District Court of New York on November 19, 1920, the
amount collected in excess of $1.00 per M cu. ft. will be subject to the further
direction of the court.
M. H. Spear, Secretary, was elected Vice-President on January 27, 1920 to succeed
L. B. Gawtry, resigned. Wm. Raynor, Assistant Secretary was elected Secretary
January 27, 1920.
No new powers or franchises were acquired,

[This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost
of perishable property (se Depreciation, page 884); but it reports on the liabilities
side of the palance sheet certain reserves ("Renewals and replacements - plant
withdrawn from service," "Contingency ") that contain accumulations made in
former years through the prescribed accounts. As no segregation is made of such
accumulations, the entire credit balances are necessarily deducted from fixed
capital or cost of property in the same manner as accumulations under the pre-
scribed account, Accrued amortization of capital representing the expired life or
amortized portion of fixed capital outlay.]

Operating Expenses.- See Table XXXIII.
Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of January 26, 1909,
this company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides
for a charge to "General amortization gas and concurrent credit to "Accrued
amortization of capital" of 12 cents per M cu. ft. sold to cover " entire amortiza-
tion, including current repairs." The company's procedure, however, is not in ac-
cordance either with this rule or with the requirements of the accounting order.
Instead it has included in operating expenses without authority from the Com-
mission the item "Renewals and replacements - plant withdrawn from service,"
charging thereto in 1920, 3¢ per M. cu. ft. of gas sold.

GAS COMPANIES AND ELECTRICAL COMPANIES
885
NEW YORK AND RICHMOND GAS COMPANY
Incorporated July 13, 1901, as a consolidation of the New York & Richmond Gas
Co., and the Richmond County Gas Light Co. The respondent is the only com-
pany supplying gas in the Borough of Richmond (1st, 2d, 3d and 4th wards).
Total number of votes cast at the last general meeting, February 7, 1921, was
11,8662. The ten largest stockholders and their share holdings as of the close of
the year, December 31, 1920, were: G. B. Dawes, 1,800; A. B. Hatcher, 1,341;
Dawes Bros., Inc., 1,125; Peoples Bank & Trust Co., 750; Claude Ashbrook, 750;
Geo. F. Goodnow, 636; Chas. A. Ward, 520; Claude Meeker, 475; Geo. P. Bruin, 375;
Jas. M. Cox, 375.
Important Changes during the Year.- No important changes, all additions to
"Fixed Capital" being minor ones. No additional powers or franchises were
acquired.

1 This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for Depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost
of perishable property (See depreciation, page 889): but it reports on the liabili-
ties side of the balance sheet certain optional reserves that contain accumulations
made in former years through the prescribed account. As no segregation is made
of such accumulations, the entire credit balances are necessarily deducted from
fixed capital or cost of property in the same manner as accumulations under the
prescribed account, Accrued amortization of capital, representing the expired life
or amortized portion of fixed capital outlay.

THE NEW YORK EDISON COMPANY
(LESSEE OF THE BRUSH ELECTRIC ILLUMINATING COMPANY)
Organized [May 21, 1901],¹ under the Transportation Corporations Law through a
consolidation agreement (dated May 1, 1901) between the New York Gas & Electric
Light, Heat & Power Company and the Edison Electric Illuminating Company of
New York. It supplies electric light and power in the boroughs of Manhattan and
The Bronx.
The company exercises sole direct control through stock ownership of the Con-
solidated Telegraph & Electric Subway Company, the Edison Light & Power
Installation Company, and the Yonkers Electric Light & Power Company (see
Miscellaneous Investments).
The Consolidated Gas Company of New York, upon the organization of the respond-
ent, acquired the right to exercise the major part of the voting power in this
company, and on December 31, 1920, was the holder of record of its 694,454 shares
of stock outstanding.
Important Changes during Year.- Stock issued: Common, $3,500,000, November 22,
1920, to pay for construction advances made by Consolidated Gas Company of New
York, February 19, 1915. Authorization by Public Service Commission September 14,
1920.
Real estate mortgages assumed: Property at 209 East 73d street, $8,000; at 38
Bowery and 8 Elizabeth street, $25,000; at 156 East 61st street, $15,000; at 122 East

& Power Co., bills receivable, at par, $50,000; fire insurance participation certifi-

A contract was entered into with the Hudson & Manhattan Railroad Co., effective
November 15, 1920, under which the operation of that company's steam generating
plant with a capacity of 28,000 kws. in Jersey City, was taken over under lease,
the major part of the current for the railway operation to be supplied over high-
tension feeders from the Waterside station.
Walter Neumuller, formerly Assistant Secretary, was elected Secretary on
February 17, 1920.
No additional powers or franchises were acquired.

2 This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost of
perishable property (See Depreciation, page 905); but it reports on the liabilities
side of the balance sheet certain reserves ("Renewals and replacements - plant
withdrawn from service," "Contingency ") that contain accumulations made in
former years through the prescribed accounts. As no segregation is made of such
accumulations, the entire credit balances are necessarily deducted from fixed
capital or cost of property in the same manner as accumulations under the pre-
scribed account, Accrued amortization of capital, representing the expired life or
amortized portion of fixed capital outlay.
3 See note 26.
4 Respondent has included among investments, $137,347.79, which it paid for the
stock of its constituent company. the Edison Electric Illuminating Co. of New
York, of a par value of $29,354: the par value is here deducted from the capital
stock liability and the balance. $107.993.79. is transferred to Suspense. Investments
have been reduced by the total of $137.347.79. [In the 1917 report the company
properly excluded this item from investments.]

Unfinished Construction and Work in Progress
DESCRIPTION OF PROJECT
First avenues, 38th and 39th streets :
Installing two 35,000 kw. turbo-generators, structural,
mechanical and electrical work; installing .compart-
ments and connections for reactance control; in-
stalling new high tension panels, feeders and equip-
ment; enlarging high tension disconnecting switch
compartments; installing feeder water heater system;
installing screen and extension of intake tunnel; in-
stalling transformer banks and equipment for rail-
road service; installing track, fire lines, hydrants, etc.,
on docks; installing steam and feed water tie lines
between Waterside stations; furnishing Flory hoist with
25 H. P. motor......
First avenue, 39th and 40th streets :
Additions and alterations to coal handling and crushing
equipment; erecting one "Harrington " forced draft
stoker; installing oil drainage piping for blower en-
gines; installing compartments and connections for
reactance control; installing compartments and equip-
ment for railroad service; building additions to trans-
former house; installing three 2,800 Kva. transformers
and connections for railroad service; installing addi-
tional high tension feeders and changing tie con-
nections
55-59 Duane street:
Installing new storage battery, structural and equipment
work; installing additional D. C. feeders; replacing
type and wire of high tension and rotary cables; rein-
forcing high tension feeder equipment; installing con-
nections for reserve transformer blower motors; erect-
ing and connecting 4,000 kw. rotary; erecting air ducts,
blowers, motors, etc., for ventilation; installing gate
locks and equipment on elevators..

-
Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting
for depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of January 21,
1909, this company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides
for a charge to "General amortization · electric and concurrent credit to
"Accrued amortization of capital of 1.4¢ per kw. hr. of low tension electric
current sold to consumers, less the amounts charged to the various repair accounts.
The company's procedure, however, is in accordance neither with this rule nor
with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead it has included in operat-
ing expenses without authority from the Commission the item Renewals and
replacements plant withdrawn from service," charging thereto 0.3¢ per kw. hr.

Main Business Office.- 124 East 15th street, New York city.
Report verified by Frederick Smith, Treasurer, March 17, 1921.
THE NEW YORK MUTUAL GAS LIGHT COMPANY
Incorporated April 17, 1866. Supplies water gas in the Borough of Manhattan
from the Battery to 53d street on the west side, and to 68th street on the east
side. The delivery of gas began in June, 1872. The Consolidated Gas Co., of New
York, which in 1905 acquired the right to exercise the major part of the voting
power in this company, held, on December 31, 1920, 16,552 shares out of 34,097 shares
outstanding.¹
Important Changes during the Year.- Rate changes instituted as follows:
"Under preliminary injunction granted by the Federal Court, a rate of $1.00
per M cu. ft. was put in effect July 1, 1920. Under a modifying order of the
court, this rate was increased to $1.10 per M cu. ft. as of December 1, 1920. These
Court Orders contain provisions for the impounding, pending final judgment, in the
action, of the amount collected in excess of the statutory rate of 80 cents per M
cu. ft. either by depositing the monies in the court or by the deposit of substituted
security." No additional powers or franchises were acquired.
[The Consolidated Gas Company reports in addition ownership of 2,310 shares
representing certificates not in its own name.]

DETAILS OF ASSETS AND LIABILITIES
Fixed Capital, December 31, 1908.- Credits during year on account of withdrawals
and retirements: Mains, $588.93; services, $7,262.55; meters, $2,495.42; manufacturing
plant, $350; total, $10,696.90. (Balances in the several subaccounts were shown in
the 1917 abstract.)
This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of cost of
perishable property (see Depreciation, page 912); but it reports on the liabilities
side of the balance sheet certain reserves (Renewals and replacements - plant
withdrawn from service: Contingency reserve) that contain accumulations made
in former years through the prescribed account. As no segregation is made of such
accumulations, the entire credit balances are necessarily deducted from paid capital
or cost of property in the same manner as accumulations under the prescribed
account, Accrued amortization of capital, representing the expired life or amortized
portion of fixed capital outlay.
Includes $42,234.02 due consumers under 80-cent gas act (20-cent rebate).
Par value of reacquired stocks.

Intercompany Purchases, Sales, etc.- Manufactured for Consolidated Gas Co.,
695,710.4 M cu. ft. at $410,923.62 (59.07¢ per M), credited to expenses, plus $41,092.37,
10% commission, credited to revenue. Purchased from New Amsterdam Gas Co.,
3,560.9 M cu. ft. at $2,561.32 (71.93¢ per M), inclusive of $232.85, 10% commission.

Sale of Residuals and By-Products.- See Table XI.
Operating Expenses.- For details, see Table XXXIII.
-
Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of March 20, 1909,
this company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides for a
charge to "General amortization
gas"
and concurrent credit to "Accrued
amortization of capital " of 18 cents per M cu. ft. sold (less the cost of actual
repairs). The company's procedure, however, is not in accordance either with this
rule or with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead it has included in
operating expenses without authority from the Commission the item Renewals and
replacements - plant withdrawn from service," charging thereto in 1920, 3¢ per M
cu. ft. of gas sold.
Taxes.- See Table XLIII.
"
Rent Charges.- Office building at 142 East 15th street, also part of east half of
fourth floor and three rooms on third floor in main building of the Consolidated
Gas Co., $19,218.72; rent of two basements, two stores and two lofts for metershop
purposes at 117-119 Mercer st., $5,000; charged to operating expenses; bulkhead
north of pier at 11th street and East river, $3,300, charged to rent deductions;
total, $27,518.72.

## p. (#913) ################################################

GAS COMPANIES AND ELECTRICAL COMPANIES
913
NORTHERN UNION GAS COMPANY.
Incorporated October 1, 1897. The company supplies mixed gas in that portion of
The Bronx formerly known as the 24th ward. It does not operate a manufacturing
plant but purchases its entire supply of gas from the Central Union Gas Co.
Stock control: The New Amsterdam Gas Co., is reported by respondent to be the
holder of record of 17,398 shares and the Central Union Gas Co., of 17,595 shares.
[The entire stock of the latter company is owned by the New Amsterdam Gas Co.,
which, in turn, is controlled through stock ownership by the Consolidated Gas
Co.]
Important Changes during the Year.- Rate changes were instituted as follows:
"Under preliminary injunction granted by the Federal Court a rate of $1 per M
cu. ft., was put into effect July 1, 1920. These court orders contain provision for the
impounding of the amount collected in excess of the statutory rate, either by de-
positing it in the court, or by deposit of substitute security." No new franchises or
powers were acquired.

1 [The report of the New Amsterdam Gas Co., shows that it owned 7.400 shares on
December 31, 1920, and that of the Central Union Gas Co., shows that it owned
7,600 shares.]
This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost
of perishable property (see Depreciation, page 917) but it reports on the lia-
bilities side of the balance sheet certain reserves ("Renewals and replacements
plant withdrawn from service"; " 'Contingency reserve") that contain accumula-
tions made in former years through the prescribed account. As no segregation is
made of such accumulations, the entire credit balances are necessarily deducted
from fixed capital or cost of property in the same manner as accumulations under
the prescribed account. Accrued amortization of capital, representing the expired
life or amortized portion of fixed capital outlay.

80¢ gas act.

Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of March 5, 1909,
this company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides for
a charge to General amortization Gas" and concurrent credit to "Accrued
amortization of capital" of 16 cents per M cu. ft. sold (less the cost of actual
repairs). The company's procedure, however, is not in accordance either with this
rule or with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead it has included
13"The volume of gas received from the Central Union Gas Co. is computed
upon the index quantities at consumers' meters. The volume of gas delivered to
the Westchester Lighting Co. is computed upon the index quantities at their con-
sumers meters and estimated quantities at public street lamps. The points of
delivery are not known.

## p. (#919) ################################################

GAS COMPANIES AND ELECTRICAL COMPANIES
919
QUEENS BOROUGH GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY
Incorporated May 29, 1902. By a certificate filed in the office of the Secretary
of State, September 19, 1902, the Town of Hempstead Gas & Electric Light Co. and
the Queens Borough Electric Light & Power Co. merged to form the Queens
Borough Gas & Electric Co. The company supplies water, gas and electric energy
in the 5th ward, Borough of Queens, City of New York, and in the southeastern
portion of the Town of Hempstead, Nassau County, including in the latter, the
incorporated villages of Lawrence, Cedarhurst, Woodsburgh, Lynbrook, Malverne,
and East Rockaway and the unincorporated villages of Inwood, Woodmere, Hewlet,
Valley Stream, and Oceanside; and has the right to operate in all of the Town of
Hempstead, with the exception of the villages of Hempstead and Garden City. Gas
was first supplied by a predecessor company about 1880-1882 and electricity in 1889.
Total number of votes cast at the last general meeting held on February 7, 1921,
was 20,000. The ten largest stockholders and their share-holdings as of the close
of the year, Dec. 31, 1920, were: G. D. Gregory, 6,650; V. Everit Macy, 4,098;
Hudson Company, 1,788; Carleton Macy, 1,700; Valentine E. Macy, Jr., 1,500; Editha
Macy, 1,500; Josiah Macy, 1,500; William Barnett, 680; James A. Mooney, 309;
Mrs. Lida M. Underhill, 100.
Important Changes during Year.- Walter P. White was elected a director of the
Company to fill the unexpired term of James A. Mooney, resigned. $34,300 Liberty
bonds were sold for $30,277.08. Real estate mortgage of $3,000 was paid off. New
base rate applicable to motors operated on lighting peak load period — 12¢ instead
of 10¢ base rate for motors used on off peak period. Discounts on both forms of
contract. No additional powers or franchises were acquired.

Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for
depreciation, see note introducing these abstracts. Under date of January 19,
1909, this company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation for the electric
department which provides for a charge to General amortization - electric of 1½
cents per kw. hr. generated which amount, less the cost of actual repairs, is con-
currently credited to "Accrued amortization of capital." It also filed at the same
time a rule for the gas department, but this rule was amended April 23, 1910,
increasing the charge to General amortization gas from 15 cents to 20 cents
per M cu. ft. of gas made. On December 14, 1918, the rule was again amended,
reducing the charge to 15 cents per M cu. ft. made, effective January 1, 1919.

RICHMOND HILL AND QUEENS COUNTY GAS LIGHT COMPANY
Incorporated February 18, 1896. The company furnishes water gas to the section
known as Richmond Hill of Ward 4, Borough of Queens, City of New York.
It has no manufacturing plant and purchases gas from the Brooklyn Union Gas
Co., which acquired its entire capital stock in January, 1897.
Important Changes during the Year. The rate paid to the Brooklyn Union Gas
Co. for gas purchased was changed from 65¢ per M cu. ft. to a price based on
the manufacturing cost of manufacture, plus transmission pumping, plus 21¢ per
M cu. ft. effective January 1, 1920.
T. P. Payne elected auditor, effective October 14, 1920, to succeed J. Gilmer,
deceased. No new powers or franchises were acquired.

DETAILS OF ASSETS AND LIABILITIES
"Fixed Capital, December 31, 1908.” — Credits during year on account of with-
drawals and retirements: Services, $11; meters, $163.43; total, $174.43. (Balances
in the several sub-accounts were shown in the 1917 abstract.)
This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost of
perishable property (see Depreciation, page 930); but it reports on the liabilities
side of the balance sheet certain reserves ("Accrued amortization of capital,"
Contingencies) that contain accumulations made in former years through the pre-
scribed account. As no segregation is made by such accumulations, the entire
credit balances are necessarily deducted from fixed capital or cost of property
in the same manner as accumulations under the prescribed account. Accrued
amortization of capital, representing the expired life or amortized portion of fixed
capital outlay.
2 All material and supplies have been transferred to the Jamaica Gas Light Co..
and material used in setting meters, etc., is furnished by the Jamaica Gas Light
Co. and charged to the respondent.

GAS COMPANIES AND ELECTRICAL COMPANIES
931
Richmond Hill and Queens County Gas Light Company
company filed with the Commission a rule of depreciation which provides for a
charge to "General amortization
- Gas" and concurrent credit to "General
amortization of capital" of 13¢ per M cu. ft. of gas sold [less the cost of actual
repairs]. The company's procedure, however, is not in accordance either with this
rule or with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead, the bulk of the
amount accumulated in the prescribed reserve is now held in an unauthorized "Con-
tingencies" reserve and the company charges
64 General amortization " and con-
currently credits "Accrued amortization of capital" with 1.5¢ per M cu. ft. of gas
sold "for the amortization of capital invested in plant withdrawn from service."
Taxes.- See Table XLIII.

payable to the Jamaica Gas Light Co., $1,250; other rent, $450; total, $1,700.

RICHMOND LIGHT AND RAILROAD COMPANY
Report of the street railway operations of this company for the year ended June
30, 1920, appears elsewhere in this volume.
Incorporated August 1, 1902 as the Richmond Light Company and on August 18,
1902 changed its name to the Richmond Light & Railroad Company [extending the
period of its corporate life and enlarging its powers to include the operation of an
electric railroad].
Respondent has sole control of the New Jersey & Staten Island Ferry Company
and 51% control of the Southfield Beach Railroad Company.
Total number of votes cast at the last general meeting, July 15, 1919, was 19,647.
The ten largest stockholders and their share-holdings as of the close of the year,
December 31, 1920, were: Leslie M. McCrum, 4,400; Edw. J. Boyd, 4,000; W. B.
Cardoza, 4,000; Thomas F. Ryan, 2,332; George B. Harvey, 1,971; N. D. Putnam,
1,836; A. B. Fletcher, 1,431; Sidney Aronstun, 900; C. B. Franklin, 719; J. K. Rice,
Jr. & Co., 550.
Important Changes during the Year.- John J. Kuhn was appointed federal
receiver April 29, 1920. No additional powers or franchises were acquired.
The respondent's emplolyees are on the combined payrolls of the following
companies: Woodhaven Gas Light Co., Jamaica Gas Light Co., and Richmond
Hill & Queens County Gas Light Co.

RIVERSIDE LIGHT AND POWER COMPANY.
Incorporated April 8, 1911. [On February 14, 1912, the company secured from the
Board of Estimate of the City of New York a franchise for a term of 14 years,
with the privilege of renewal for a further period of ten years. This franchise
gives the company the right to supply electricity in the territory bounded by 129th
street, 134th street, Twelfth avenue and N. Y. C. & H. R. R. R Co., to Twelfth
avenue. It does not generate current, but receives it under verbal agreement from
the Riverside Cold Storage Co.] Its stock is owned by three persons, whose share-
holdings as of the close of the year, Dec. 31, 1920, were: Joseph Conron, 24½;
John E. Conron, 24½; Chas. F. Kelley, 1.
Important Changes During the Year.- None. No additional franchises or powers
were acquired.
