THE STANDARD GAS LIGHT COMPANY OF THE CITY OF NEW YORK
Certificate of incorporation filed February 11, 1886. Stock control of the com-
pany was acquired by the Consolidated Gas Co. of New York in May, 1905. Respond-
ent reports it to be the holder of 40,693 shares of preferred stock out of a total
outstanding of 41,625 shares and 47,950 shares of common stock out of a total of
49,160 shares outstanding.¹
Important Changes during the Year.- Under preliminary injunction granted by
the Federal Court a rate of 1.00 per M cu. ft. was put in effect July 1, 1920.
Modifying order increased this rate to $1.10 on December 1, 1920. These court
orders contain provisions for the impounding of the amount collected in excess
[The Consolidated Gas Co. reports in addition ownership of 328 shares of pre-
ferred stock and 48 shares of common stock representing certificates not in its
own name.]

2 This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost
of perishable property (see Depreciation, page 946); but it reports on the lia-
bilities side of the balance sheet certain reserves ("Renewals and replacements,
plant withdrawn from service; Contingency reserve") that contain accumula-
tions made in former years through the prescribed account. As no segregation is
made of such accumulations, the entire credit balances are necessarily deducted
from fixed capital or cost of property in the same manner as accumulations under
the prescribed account. Accrued amortization of capital, representing the expired
life or amortized portion of fixed capital outlay.

THE UNITED ELECTRIC LIGHT AND POWER COMPANY
Incorporated February 3, 1887. Supplies electric energy in the Borough of Man-
hattan [For corporate history see Annual report, 1910, Vol. I, 247-252.]
On December 31, 1920, the Consolidated Gas Co. was the holder of record of
16,415 shares of preferred stock and 34,984 shares of common stock out of a total
of 52,993 shares outstanding.
Important Changes during Year.- Issue to the Consolidated Gas Co. of $8,825,000,
7% debentures in discharge of its demand notes and demand loans. No new powers
or franchises were acquired.

[This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e., amortization of the cost of
perishable property (see Depreciation, page 955): but it reports on the liabilities
side of the balance sheet certain reserves (Renewals and replacements - plant
withdrawn from service: Contingency reserve) that contain accumulations made in
former years through the prescribed account. As no segregation is made of such
accumulations, the entire credit balances are neecssarily deducted from fixed
capital or cost of property in the same manner as accumulations under the pre-
scribed account. Accrued amortization of capital, representing the expired life or
amortized portion of fixed capital outlay.]

a charge to "General amortization - electric." The rule failed to state that the
concurrent credit would be made "Accrued amortization of capital." The company's
procedure, however, is not in accordance either with this rule or with the require-
ments of the accounting order. Instead, it has included in operating expenses
without authority from the Commission the item "Renewals and replacements
plant withdrawn from service" charging thereto in 1919, and 1920, 0.3¢ per kw.hr.
of electric current sold to general consumers.

PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION - FIRST DISTRICT
THE WOODHAVEN GAS LIGHT COMPANY
Incorporated August 30, 1871. The company furnishes gas to ward 4, Borough of
Queens, City of New York [with exception of the former villages of Jamaica and
Richmond Hill]. It has no manufacturing plant, and purchases gas from the
Brooklyn Union Gas Co, which acquired its entire capital stock in July, 1897.
Important Changes during the Year. The rate paid to the Brooklyn Union Gas
Company for gas purchased was changed from 65¢ per M to a price based on the
manufacturing cost of the Brooklyn Union Gas Co., plus transmission pumping plus
21 cents per M, effective January 1, 1920. J. Gilmer, Auditor, deceased July 9, 1920;
succeeded by T. P. Payne, October 14, 1920. No additional powers or franchises
were acquired.

This company no longer observes the accounting procedure prescribed by the
Commission respecting provision for depreciation, i. e.. amortization of the cost
of perishable property (see Depreciation, page 959); but it reports on the lia-
bilities side of the balance sheet certain reserves ("Accrued amortization of
capital" and "Contingencies") that contain accumulations made in former years
through the prescribed account. As no segregation is made of such accumulations.
the entire credit balances are necessarily deducted from fixed capital or cost of
property in the same manner as accumulations under the prescribed account,
Accrued amortization of capital, representing the expired life or amortized portion
of fixed capital outlay.

Light Co. and charged to the respondent.

Depreciation.- For this Commission's requirements concerning the accounting for

..
-
..
of 13 cents per M cu. ft. sold (less the cost of actual
repairs). The company's procedure, however, is not in accordance either with
this rule or with the requirements of the accounting order. Instead, the bulk
of the amount accumulated in the prescribed reserve is now held in an unauthorized
"Contingencies" reserve, and the company charges General amortization" and
concurrently credits "Accrued amortization of capital" with 1.5¢ per M cu. ft. of
gas sold for the amortization of capital invested in plant withdrawn from
service."

## p. (#962) ################################################

962

PUBLIC SERVICE COMMISSION - FIRST DISTRICT
THE NEW YORK STEAM COMPANY
OPERATED BY RECEIVERS
Incorporated September 19, 1881, under the General Corporation Law as a con-
solidation of The Steam Heating & Power Co. of New York and the New York
Steam Co. The company began furnishing steam to private consumers in Man-
hattan in May, 1882, and to the City in January, 1888. It has stock control of the
United States Mineral Wool Co.
Stock control over the New York Steam Co. is held by the Andrews Institute
for Girls of Willoughby, Ohio, which owns a majority of the stock outstanding
acquired in 1910 as legatees of Wallace C. Andrews. In 1913 the Institute owned
$3,478,200 of $5,431,956 capital stock outstanding. From December, 1914 to 1918, control
over the respondent was exercised by E. G. Stevenson and R. G. Hanford.
[On the application of the Institute, Wm. C. Fitts, George F. Hurd, and G. C.
St. John were appointed receivers on August 19, 1918, by the U. S. District Court.
The court records of the hearing show that the Andrews Institute, in addition to
its ownership of capital stock, owned $2,396,000 refunding mortgage bonds of a
total of $2,450,000 outstanding. It also owned real estate mortgages amounting to
$210,500. The Independent Order of Foresters of Canada owns $2,062,000, or the
entire amount outstanding of the general mortgage bonds issued in 1916.]
Important Changes during the Year.- Receivers' certificates, par value, $612,000
issued as of June 1, 1920 and November 15, 1920 to finance business and to store
coal over winter months; sold at 97.90, 99, and par. No new powers or franchises
were acquired.
NEW SCHEDULE OF RATES
The receivers of the company filed with the Commission new schedules of rates,
effective May 1, 1920, and September 1, 1920, in which principal rates were as
follows":

19 In answer to the request for an explanation of the character of this item the
company, replied, October 17, 1916: "We have made no detailed analysis of the
Contingent Asset Account up to the present. We understand that the accountants
for the Commission have made such an analysis in connection with our Capitaliza-
tion Case No. 2036." In the analysis referred to, Exhibit R, Case No. 2036, it is
stated that " on April 30, 1896, the accounts City grants and patents' and general
expenses construction' were closed into contingent assets' account by the follow-
ing journal entry: An inventory of the assets of the company having been taken
May 1, 1896, by the executive committee in accordance with instructions to it by
the Board of Trustees at the board meetings on June 13, 1893, and June 11, 1895,
the accounts of 'city grants and patents" and "general expenses con-
struction," representing assets of contingent and doubtful value, are, by order of
the executive committee of the company, hereby transferred to "contingent assets "
account. The above action of the executive committee has been sanctioned and
ratified by the Board of Trustees at its meeting June 9, 1896.'"

Depreciation. Charges are made to Depreciation expense and credits to deprecia-
tion reserve monthly on the basis of 2¢ per M pounds of steam sold during the
previous month. The credits to reserve are distributed as follows: 13% applicable
to station B; 7% to station J; 12% to station JX; 0.5% to station B shop; 0.5%
to station J shop; 18% to District B street system; 25% to District J street sys-
tem; 2% to patterns; 11% to floating equipment; 1% to building at 177 Washington
street; 1% to Transportation plant and equipment; 4% to auto trucks (coal); 1% to
utility equipment; 1% to Office furniture and fixtures; 3% to shop street and station
tools.

MISCELLANEOUS INFORMATION
Plant Equipment in Place.- Steam station B, located at 170-174 Greenwich street,
contains the following boilers: 56 Babcock and Wilcox, of 250 h. p. each; 1 Parker,
of 312 h. p., and 1 Climax, of 1,000 h. p.
Steam station J located on East river between 50th and 60th streets, contains
the following boilers: 8 Climax, of 1,000 h. p. each; 1 Climax, of 750 h. p. ; 4 Altman
and Taylor, of 510 h. p. each; and 3 Babcock and Wilcox, of 300 h. p. each.
Steam station J addition, located on East river between 58th and 59th streets,
contains the following boilers: 8 Babcock and Wilcox, of 600 h. p. each, and 6
Edgemoor (with stokers), of 700 h. p. each.
Steam station A, located at 2 Burling slip, contains the following boilers: 12 Edge-
moor boilers with Cox stokers, 1,043 h. p. each.
Coal Consumed and Steam Sold.- District B: Total coal consumed, 194,483 tons;
decrease from last year, 14,281 tons. Steam sold (1,000 lbs.): General service, 601,624;
annual power, 1,050,873: apartment house, 2.341; summer power, 201,311; total, 1,856,-
149; decrease from last year, 111,964.
District J: Total coal consumed, 97,915 tons; decrease from last year, 20,162.
Steam sold (1,000 lbs.): General service, 498,118; annual power, 50,764; apartment
house, 234,363; total, 783,245; decrease from last year, 185,787.

uncollectible bills are not included in operating expenses.

property leased, paid by respondent and charged to coal stock account as part of

## p. (#979) ################################################

INDEX OF COMPANIES
In this volume the returns from street railways and other common carriers are presented for the
most part in tabulations; other data (relating chiefly to the corporate organizations and inter-
corporate relationships) appear in the introduction to the tabulations. Page references hereunder,
in the case of common carriers, pertain to such data; in the case of light, heat and power corpora-
tions, to the abstracts containing the financial statements, etc. in the form heretofore used. The
index also contains references to a small number of companies that have filed returns as inchoate
or dormant corporations (not published herein).
The history of railroad and street railroad corporations that have at any time operated in the
First District or were organized to operate therein is contained in Volume V of the Commission's
Annual Report for 1913, to which reference may be made for information concerning companies
not listed below.
